  • Private Boat Hire Marbella: Exclusive Charters with Skipper
  • Catamaran Charter Estepona: Relaxed Sailing on the Costa del Sol

faqs.q: Do I need a licence to rent a speedboat in Benalmadena Marina?
faqs.a: No. If the boat has a 20hp or less outboard engine, Spanish law allows you to drive it without any licence. You must be over 18 and have valid ID. The rental operator will give you a 5-minute briefing and you are good to go.

faqs.q: How much does speedboat hire in Benalmadena Marina cost?
faqs.a: Prices start from around EUR 120 for two hours in low season and go up to EUR 400 for a full day in high season. Fuel is usually included for the first hour, then extra at around EUR 10 per litre. The deposit is EUR 200-500, refundable.

faqs.q: Can I take the boat to Puerto Banús or Marbella?
faqs.a: No. The licence-free boats are restricted to a defined zone around Benalmadena, usually from Punta de Torrox to Torreblanca. Going to Puerto Banús (15 nautical miles) is not allowed because of fuel range and insurance limits.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad on my booking day?
faqs.a: The operator will cancel or reschedule if the wind is above 15 knots or the sea is rough. You get a full refund or a new date. Check the weather forecast before booking, and choose a flexible cancellation policy.

faqs.q: How many people can fit on a licence-free speedboat?
faqs.a: Most boats are rated for 4 to 6 passengers, including the driver. The actual capacity depends on the boat model and the operator. For comfort, 4 adults is the maximum. More than that and it gets cramped.

faqs.q: Do I need to speak Spanish to rent the boat?
faqs.a: No. The operators in Benalmadena Marina speak English. The briefing, the rental agreement, and the radio communications are all in English. You only need basic English to understand the safety instructions.

faqs.q: Can I fish from the speedboat?
faqs.a: Yes, you can fish from the boat, but you need a fishing licence from the Junta de Andalucía. You can buy one online for a few euros. The boat does not come with fishing gear, so bring your own rod and tackle.

title: Luxury Yacht Rental Puerto Banus Weekend: 2-Day Deal
metaDescription: Book a luxury yacht rental Puerto Banus weekend for 2 days. Motor yachts, catamarans, skippers included. Compare deals from EUR 2,500. Reserve now.
h1: Luxury Yacht Rental Puerto Banus Weekend: 2-Day Deal
keyword: luxury yacht rental Puerto Banus weekend
intro:
You want a weekend on the water without the hassle of ownership. A luxury yacht rental Puerto Banus weekend is the obvious move: two full days, a professional skipper, and a boat that turns heads in the marina. We run through exactly what you get, which ports work best, and how to avoid the tourist traps.

BoatHire24 lists verified boats in Marbella, Puerto Banus, Estepona, and Sotogrande. Every charter includes a licensed skipper, so you can focus on the sun, the sea, and that bottle of cava. No hidden fees, no surprise fuel bills.
bodyHtml:
What a 2-Day Luxury Yacht Rental Includes

A weekend charter from Puerto Banus typically runs from Friday afternoon to Sunday evening, or Saturday morning to Monday morning. You get the boat for roughly 48 hours, with the skipper on board for around 8 to 10 hours per day. Overnight stays are allowed on most yachts, though some catamarans charge extra for bedding. The skipper sleeps in a separate cabin or returns to shore, depending on the agreement.

Standard inclusions: fuel for 4 to 6 hours of cruising per day, port fees for the base marina, insurance, and the skipper’s fee. Extras like food, drinks, and land excursions cost extra. Some operators offer a provisioning service, so you can pre-order champagne and tapas for arrival. Expect to pay from around EUR 2,500 for a 40-foot motor yacht, up to EUR 8,000 or more for a 60-foot flybridge with a jacuzzi.

Puerto Banus: The Weekend Playground

Puerto Banus is the obvious starting point for a luxury weekend. You have the marina bars, the boutiques, and the constant parade of superyachts. Departure from here means you can cruise east towards Marbella's beaches or west towards Estepona. The port itself is a spectacle, but the real value is the quick access to the open sea. Within 20 minutes you are past the breakwater and heading for the Marbella coastline.

The main drawback: weekend traffic in and out of the marina can be chaotic. Book a morning departure before 10 am to avoid the queue. Also, check the wind forecast. The Poniente wind can kick up in the afternoon, making the return bumpy. A good skipper will adjust the route accordingly.

Other Departure Ports Worth Considering

If you want a quieter weekend, consider leaving from Marbella Marina or Cabopino. Marbella Marina is closer to the old town and the beach clubs, and it is less crowded than Banus. Cabopino, near the Artola dunes, is a small, family-friendly port with a decent chiringuito. Estepona is further west and offers access to the Strait of Gibraltar, where you can spot dolphins. Sotogrande is more exclusive, with a golf crowd and a longer run to the sea. Benalmadena, east of Marbella, is a solid option if you want to combine the weekend with a visit to the marina's restaurants.

Each port has its own vibe. Banus is for showing off. Marbella is for relaxed elegance. Estepona is for nature. Your choice affects the cruising range and the scenery. A weekend from Sotogrande, for example, gives you a chance to visit Gibraltar or even cross to Ceuta if the weather is calm.

Boat Options for a Weekend Charter

Motor yachts dominate the weekend market. A 40 to 50-foot Sunseeker or Azimut is the default choice: fast, stable, and with a decent flybridge for sunbathing. Catamarans are popular for groups of 8 to 12 people, offering more deck space and shallower draft for anchoring close to shore. Sailing yachts are cheaper but slower, so you cover less ground. RIBs and speedboats are for day trips, not weekends, as they lack cabins and comfort. Fishing boats are available if you want to combine the weekend with angling. Licence-free day boats are an option for small groups, but they are limited to coastal cruising and have no skipper.

Superyachts, over 80 feet, are available on request. Prices start from around EUR 15,000 for a weekend, with a full crew. These are for serious celebrations or corporate events. Check the search page for current availability.

Pricing Guidance for a Weekend Deal

Prices vary by season, boat size, and itinerary. Low season (November to March) weekends start from around EUR 2,500 for a 40-foot motor yacht. Mid-season (April to June, September to October) runs EUR 3,500 to EUR 5,500. High season (July and August) pushes prices to EUR 6,000 or more for the same boat. Catamarans are generally 20 to 30 percent cheaper than motor yachts of similar length. Sailing yachts are 30 to 50 percent cheaper.

Fuel is usually included for a set number of hours. If you want to cruise more than 6 hours per day, expect a surcharge of around EUR 150 per hour for a 40-foot boat. Food and drink can be arranged through the charter company or brought onboard. Some companies offer a flat rate for the weekend, including all costs except tips. Always ask for a written breakdown before booking.

Best Time of Year for a Weekend Charter

May, June, and September are the sweet spots. You get warm weather, 25 to 30 degrees C, and the sea is calm. July and August are hot, 35 degrees C on deck, and the marinas are packed. Book at least two weeks in advance for summer weekends. October and November are still pleasant, but the wind picks up. December to February is risky: you might get a sunny day, but the water is cold and the sea can be rough. Some boats are not available in winter.

For a weekend, you want at least 20 degrees C and light winds. Check the AEMET forecast for Marbella three days before departure. If the Levante wind is blowing, stay in port or choose a sheltered route along the coast.

Sample Weekend Itinerary from Puerto Banus

Day one: depart Puerto Banus at 10 am, cruise east to Cala del Faro, anchor for lunch and swimming. Then head to Marbella Marina for a late afternoon drink at the Club del Mar. Overnight at anchor or return to Banus. Day two: cruise west to Estepona, stop at the old town for a walk and lunch at a chiringuito on the beach. Return to Banus by 6 pm. This route covers about 40 nautical miles total, with plenty of stops. The skipper handles the navigation, so you just relax.

Alternative: head south from Banus towards the Strait of Gibraltar. You might see dolphins, and if the weather is calm, you can anchor near the coast of Africa. This is a longer run, about 25 NM one way, so you need a fast boat. A sailing yacht would not make it in a weekend.

How to Book a 2-Day Weekend Deal

Go to BoatHire24 and use the search filters: set dates, choose Puerto Banus or another port, select motor yacht or catamaran, and tick the “weekend” option. Compare listings by total price, not just the base rate. Contact the owner or broker directly via the platform to confirm availability and ask about the cancellation policy. Most weekend charters require a 50 percent deposit and the balance 14 days before departure. Payment by bank transfer or credit card is standard.

Read the terms carefully. Some charters charge extra for cleaning, port fees outside the base, or late return. The skipper’s tip is not included, usually 10 to 15 percent of the charter fee. Book at least a month in advance for July and August. For last-minute deals, check the blog for cancellations and off-peak offers.

Who the Weekend Charter Is For

Couples looking for a romantic escape. Groups of friends celebrating a birthday or stag do. Families with older children who want space and privacy. Corporate teams on a bonding trip. The weekend deal works best for people who want to maximise time on the water without the cost of a full week. It is not for budget travellers or those who get seasick easily.

If you are a solo traveller, consider joining a shared charter. Some operators offer weekend slots on a catamaran with other guests, which lowers the cost. Ask the broker when you enquire.

Local Tips for a Smooth Weekend

Book a skipper who knows the local anchorages. The best spots are often hidden behind rocks or in small coves. Ask the charter company for recommendations on restaurants that deliver to the boat. In Puerto Banus, the fish market at the port sells fresh catch in the morning. Buy some and ask the skipper to grill it for lunch. Bring sunscreen, a hat, and a light jacket for the evening. The wind can drop the temperature by 10 degrees C after sunset.

Check the boat’s insurance covers you for swimming and water sports. Some policies exclude jet skis or towed toys. If you want to use a paddleboard or snorkel gear, confirm it is included or rent it separately.

Why a Weekend Deal Beats a Single Day Charter

A one-day charter from Puerto Banus costs roughly the same as the first day of a two-day deal, but you lose the second day. The weekend package gives you two full days of cruising, an overnight stay on the water, and the chance to visit two or three different spots. You also avoid the rush to return by sunset. On a day charter, you are back at the marina by 7 pm. On a weekend, you can anchor overnight at Cala del Faro or off the coast of Estepona and wake up to the sunrise over the Mediterranean. The cost per hour drops significantly, often by 30 to 40 percent compared to a single day. For a group of six, the weekend deal works out cheaper than two separate day charters.

The Hidden Costs of a Weekend Charter

Base price is not the final price. You need to budget for food and drink, which is not included. A weekend’s worth of groceries for six people costs around EUR 200 to EUR 400, depending on how much champagne and steak you want. Some charter companies offer a provisioning service, but they mark up the prices by 15 to 20 percent. You can bring your own supplies, but the skipper may charge a fee for loading and storing. Another hidden cost is the overnight port fee if you choose to dock instead of anchoring. Marinas like Puerto Banus charge around EUR 50 to EUR 100 per night for a 40-foot boat. Anchoring is free, but you need calm weather. Finally, the fuel surcharge for extra cruising hours can add EUR 300 to EUR 600 if you want to cover more than the standard 6 hours per day. Ask for a full breakdown before you pay the deposit.

Best Boat Types for a Two-Day Weekend

Motor yachts in the 40 to 50-foot range are the most practical. They have enough cabin space for four to six people to sleep comfortably, a flybridge for sunbathing, and a decent cruising speed of 20 to 25 knots. Catamarans, like a Lagoon 42, offer more deck space and a shallower draft, which means you can anchor closer to shore. They are also more stable at anchor, so you sleep better. The downside is they are slower, around 8 to 10 knots, so you cover less distance. Sailing yachts are cheaper but require more planning. You need wind to move, and on a calm weekend you might motor the whole time, which defeats the purpose. RIBs and speedboats are not suitable for weekends because they lack cabins. Superyachts over 80 feet are overkill for a weekend unless you are hosting a party. For most groups, a 45-foot motor yacht or a 50-foot catamaran is the sweet spot.

Specific Anchorage Spots for Overnight Stays

Cala del Faro, just east of Puerto Banus, is the best overnight spot for a weekend. It is sheltered from the Poniente wind, has a sandy bottom for anchoring, and is quiet at night. You can see the lights of the Marbella hills from the deck. Another option is the bay off the Río Verde, but it can get choppy if the wind shifts. West of Banus, the coast near Estepona has several coves, like the one behind the Estepona Marina breakwater. This spot is protected and close to the old town for a morning walk. If you want privacy, head to the area between Sotogrande and Gibraltar. The anchorage at Punta Chullera is remote and has clear water for snorkelling. Avoid anchoring near the main shipping lanes off Gibraltar, as the wakes from large vessels can rock the boat all night. The skipper will know the best spots for the current weather conditions.

Weather Risks for a Weekend Charter

The Mediterranean in summer is usually calm, but the wind can change quickly. The Poniente wind, from the west, picks up in the afternoon and can reach 20 to 25 knots. This makes the return to Puerto Banus bumpy, especially on a smaller boat. The Levante wind, from the east, is less common but brings rough seas and reduced visibility. Check the AEMET forecast for the Costa del Sol three days before your charter. If the wind is forecast to be over 15 knots, consider choosing a catamaran, which handles better in choppy conditions. Alternatively, plan a route that stays close to the coast, where the sea is calmer. If the forecast is bad, the charter company may cancel or reschedule. Most contracts have a weather clause that allows a full refund if the skipper deems it unsafe. Do not push for a departure if the conditions are marginal; it is not worth the discomfort or the risk.

How to Split Costs for a Group Weekend

For a group of six to eight people, the weekend deal becomes very affordable. Assume a total cost of EUR 4,000 for a 45-foot motor yacht in mid-season. Split six ways, that is EUR 667 per person. Add EUR 100 per person for food and drink, and EUR 50 per person for the skipper tip, and the total per person is around EUR 817. For eight people, it drops to EUR 613 per person. Compare that to a hotel weekend in Marbella, where a decent room costs EUR 200 per night plus meals, and the boat starts to look like a bargain. The key is to agree on the budget before booking. One person should collect the money upfront to avoid chasing people later. Use a payment app like Revolut or Bizum for easy transfers. If someone drops out, have a backup guest or a cancellation policy that covers the loss.

Weekend Charter for Special Occasions

A weekend yacht rental from Puerto Banus works well for birthdays, anniversaries, and small weddings. For a birthday, you can arrange for a private chef to cook on board, or pre-order a cake and decorations. Some charter companies allow you to bring a DJ or a small sound system, but check the noise restrictions. Marinas enforce quiet hours after 11 pm, so keep the volume down if you dock overnight. For a wedding, the weekend gives you time for a ceremony on deck, a reception at anchor, and a night of celebration. You can also use the boat as accommodation for the couple or the guests. Corporate events, like a team-building weekend or a client entertainment trip, are also common. The boat provides a private setting for meetings during the day and socialising in the evening. Ask the broker about catering options for larger groups.

Last-Minute Weekend Deals and Discounts

If you are flexible, you can find last-minute weekend deals at a discount. Charter companies sometimes reduce prices by 20 to 30 percent if the boat is still unsold three days before the weekend. Check the search page and filter by availability for the current weekend. You might also find cancellations on the blog, where brokers post flash sales. The downside is you have less choice of boats and ports. You might end up with a smaller motor yacht or a catamaran that is not your first choice. But if you are just looking for a weekend on the water, it is a good way to save money. Book early in the day, as these deals go fast. Have your payment details ready and be prepared to confirm within a few hours.

What to Pack for a Weekend on a Yacht

Space on a 40-foot yacht is limited. Pack light: one small bag per person. Essentials include swimwear, a towel, sunscreen (SPF 50), a hat, and sunglasses. Bring a light jumper or fleece for the evenings, as the temperature drops. Non-slip shoes are useful on deck, but bare feet are fine on most boats. If you plan to eat on board, bring reusable plates and cutlery, though most charters provide basic kitchenware. A cooler bag for drinks is handy if the boat’s fridge is small. Do not bring glass bottles; they are a hazard on deck. The skipper will have a first aid kit, but bring any personal medication. If you are prone to seasickness, take a motion sickness pill an hour before departure. Finally, bring a waterproof phone case for photos on the water.

Legal and Safety Considerations for a Weekend Charter

All charters on BoatHire24 include a licensed skipper, so you do not need a boating licence. The skipper is responsible for safety, navigation, and compliance with Spanish maritime law. They will give a safety briefing at the start of the charter, including the location of life jackets, fire extinguishers, and the emergency radio. You must follow their instructions, especially in rough weather. The boat is insured, but the policy may have exclusions for reckless behaviour, like jumping from the flybridge or ignoring the skipper’s orders. Alcohol is allowed on board, but the skipper must remain sober. If you plan to swim, the skipper will anchor in a safe area away from boat traffic. Do not swim near the propellers or in marinas. For children, life jackets must be worn at all times on deck. The charter company should provide child-sized jackets on request.

faqs.a: A 2-day charter includes the boat, a licensed skipper, fuel for 4-6 hours cruising per day, port fees for the base marina, and insurance. Food, drinks, and extra fuel hours cost extra. Some boats include paddleboards or snorkel gear.

faqs.q: How much does a weekend yacht rental in Puerto Banus cost?
faqs.a: Prices start from around EUR 2,500 for a 40-foot motor yacht in low season, rising to EUR 6,000 or more in August. Catamarans are cheaper, superyachts are price on request. Always ask for a full breakdown.

faqs.a: Yes, most motor yachts and catamarans allow overnight stays. The skipper usually sleeps in a separate cabin or returns to shore. Confirm bedding and any extra fees when booking.

faqs.a: No. All charters on BoatHire24 include a licensed skipper who handles navigation. You do not need a licence or experience.

faqs.a: Day one: cruise east to Cala del Faro and Marbella Marina. Day two: west to Estepona old town. This covers 40 NM and includes swimming, lunch stops, and sightseeing.

faqs.q: Can I cancel a weekend charter?
faqs.a: Cancellation policies vary. Most require a 50% deposit and full payment 14 days before departure. Check the terms on the listing or contact the broker directly.

title: Private Boat Hire Marbella Fishing: Charter a Rod
metaDescription: Book private boat hire Marbella fishing with a licensed skipper. Rods, bait, and cold drinks included. Depart from Puerto Banús, Marbella Marina, or Cabopino.
h1: Private Boat Hire Marbella Fishing: Charter a Rod
intro:
You want to fish the Costa del Sol without the hassle of bringing your own gear or figuring out where the fish are biting. Private boat hire Marbella fishing gives you a licensed skipper who knows the local marks, a boat kitted out with rods and bait, and the freedom to spend a few hours or a full day on the water. No crowds, no queuing for a spot on a party boat. Just you, your crew, and the Mediterranean.

BoatHire24 connects you with skippers who operate from Puerto Banús, Marbella Marina, Cabopino, Estepona, Sotogrande, and Benalmádena. You choose the boat type, the duration, and whether you want to target bream off the rocks or chase tuna in the deeper water. The skipper handles the navigation, the licences, and the local knowledge. You just turn up and cast.
bodyHtml:
What You Get with Private Boat Hire Marbella Fishing

When you book a private fishing charter through BoatHire24, the boat comes with a licensed skipper who handles everything except the rod in your hand. The boat is fully equipped for fishing: rods, reels, tackle, bait, fishfinder electronics, and safety gear. You also get cold drinks, ice boxes for your catch, and a cooler with water and soft drinks. Some charters throw in a light lunch or tapas. You do not need a fishing licence because the skipper holds the commercial one. You just need to show up with sun cream, a hat, and a camera. The skipper will clean and fillet your catch if you want to take it back to your holiday apartment or a local restaurant that cooks what you caught.

Which Ports You Can Depart From

Private boat hire Marbella fishing covers the whole coast from Benalmádena in the east to Sotogrande in the west. Each port gives you access to different fishing grounds. From Puerto Banús you can reach the rocky reefs off Cala del Faro and the deeper water south of La Concha mountain in under 20 minutes. Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is the closest point for the artificial reefs and the sandy banks off the Río Verde estuary. Cabopino is a quieter option with direct access to the Cabopino marine reserve. Estepona gives you quick runs to the Strait of Gibraltar, where the current pushes baitfish and dolphins close to shore. Sotogrande is the best departure point for the Alboran Sea and the deeper canyons where big game fish hang out. Benalmádeña is handy if you are staying on the eastern side of the Costa del Sol, with good bottom fishing around the Fuengirola wrecks.

Boat Types Available for Fishing Charters

You can book different types of boats depending on how many people you have and what kind of fishing you want to do. Motor yachts from around 35 to 50 feet offer a stable platform, cabin space for shade, and a decent cruising speed to reach distant marks. They work well for groups of 6 to 10 people who want a mix of fishing and cruising. Catamarans are even more stable, with a wide beam that reduces roll in choppy water. They are good for families with children or anyone prone to seasickness. Sailing yachts are less common for fishing, but you can find a few skippers who combine a sail with trolling for dorado or tuna. RIBs and speedboats are the fastest option, reaching 30 to 40 knots, so you can cover ground quickly and fish multiple spots in a half-day charter. They are best for 2 to 4 anglers who want a sporty experience. Fishing boats are purpose-built with rod holders, live bait wells, and outriggers. They range from 20 to 30 feet and are the most cost-effective option for serious anglers. Licence-free day boats are small motorboats you can drive yourself without a boating licence, but they are limited to inshore waters and light tackle fishing. For the full private boat hire Marbella fishing experience, most people go with a motor yacht or a purpose-built fishing boat with a skipper.

Pricing Guidance for Private Fishing Charters

Prices vary by boat size, duration, and the number of people on board. A half-day charter (4 hours) on a 25-foot fishing boat for up to 4 people costs from around EUR 400 to EUR 600. A full day (8 hours) on the same boat runs from EUR 700 to EUR 1,000. For a larger motor yacht with cabin space and a crew of two, a half-day for 6 to 8 people is typically from EUR 1,200 to EUR 1,800. Full-day rates for the same yacht go from EUR 2,000 to EUR 3,000. Catamarans tend to be slightly more expensive because of the extra stability and space. RIBs and speedboats are cheaper, often from EUR 350 for a half-day for 2 people. All prices include the skipper, fuel, fishing gear, bait, and refreshments. You should budget an extra 10 to 15 percent for a tip if the skipper works hard to put you on the fish. You can compare options and check availability on the BoatHire24 search page.

Best Seasons for Fishing off Marbella

The Costa del Sol has year-round fishing, but the species change with the seasons. Spring (March to May) is good for bream, sea bass, and mackerel as the water warms up. Summer (June to September) is the peak season for game fish like bluefin tuna, dorado, and amberjack. The water temperature hits 24 to 26 degrees Celsius, and the fish follow the baitfish close to shore. Autumn (October to November) brings good bottom fishing for grouper and snapper, plus the start of the swordfish season in deeper water. Winter (December to February) is quieter for sport fishing, but you can still catch hake, whiting, and the occasional tope shark. The Strait of Gibraltar is productive all year for those willing to run 10 to 15 nautical miles offshore. If you want the best chance of landing a big tuna, book a private boat hire Marbella fishing charter between June and September. For a relaxed day of bottom fishing with the family, spring and autumn are ideal.

How to Book Your Private Fishing Charter

Booking through BoatHire24 is straightforward. You go to the BoatHire24 homepage, select your preferred port, the number of people, and the date. The search results show available boats with their specifications, photos, and prices. You can filter by boat type, duration, and amenities like onboard toilet or cabin. Once you pick a boat, you send a booking request through the platform. The skipper confirms availability and sends you a payment link. You pay a deposit of around 30 percent to secure the date, and the balance is due on the day of the charter. You can also contact the BoatHire24 team by phone or email if you want a custom itinerary, like a full-day trip to the Strait of Gibraltar or a night fishing session under the stars. Cancellation policies vary by skipper, but most offer a full refund if you cancel at least 48 hours in advance.

Who Private Boat Hire Marbella Fishing Is For

This type of charter works for several kinds of people. It is ideal for holidaymakers who want to try fishing without buying gear or dealing with licences. It suits experienced anglers who want access to local knowledge and a well-equipped boat without owning one. It also works for corporate groups or stag parties who want a day on the water with a bit of competition over who catches the biggest fish. Families with children are welcome on most boats, and skippers are used to teaching beginners how to cast and reel. If you are a solo traveller, you can book a private charter for yourself, though you pay the full boat rate. Some skippers offer shared trips if you want to split the cost with strangers, but private boat hire Marbella fishing is exactly that: private, so you control the guest list.

What You Might Catch on a Marbella Fishing Charter

The Mediterranean off Marbella is richer than many people expect. Inshore, you can catch gilthead bream, sea bass, red mullet, and mackerel. Further out, around the artificial reefs and the rocky bottom near Cala del Faro, you find dentex, grouper, and amberjack. In the summer, bluefin tuna pass through the Strait of Gibraltar and into the Alboran Sea, and some charters target them with live bait or trolling lures. Dorado (mahi-mahi) show up in late summer, often around floating debris or weed lines. Swordfish are caught at night in the deeper water off Estepona and Sotogrande. You also get the occasional tope shark, smooth-hound, and stingray. The skipper will advise on the best technique for the day based on the conditions, whether that is bottom fishing, trolling, or jigging. You can keep your catch within legal limits, and the skipper will help you with the regulations.

Local Colour and Things to Do After Fishing

After your private boat hire Marbella fishing charter, you are never far from something worth doing. If you depart from Puerto Banús, you can walk around the marina and look at the superyachts, then grab a plate of grilled fish at one of the harbourfront restaurants. From Marbella Marina, you are a few minutes from the old town with its orange-tree-lined streets and tapas bars. Cabopino has a quiet beach and a handful of chiringuitos where you can eat your catch if you ask the chef to cook it. Estepona old town is worth a visit for the painted flower pots and the plaza de las flores. Sotogrande is more upmarket, with golf courses and a yacht club. If you have time, a day trip to the Nerja caves is about an hour's drive east, or you can drive west to the Strait of Gibraltar and watch the dolphins from Tarifa. The BoatHire24 blog has more ideas for combining fishing with sightseeing on the Costa del Sol.

Why Private Hire Beats a Group Fishing Trip

You might see cheaper prices for shared fishing trips where you join strangers on a boat. Those are fine if you just want to wet a line. But private boat hire Marbella fishing means you decide the itinerary, the pace, and the guest list. You are not stuck waiting for someone else to reel in a tangle. You can ask the skipper to move to a different spot if the fish are not biting. You can take a break, swim in a quiet cove, or eat lunch on deck without a schedule. For a group of four or more, the per-person cost of a private charter often works out close to a shared trip, especially if you split the full-day rate. You also get the skipper’s full attention, which matters if you are learning or want tips on local techniques.

Specific Fishing Spots You Can Target from Each Port

From Puerto Banús, the reef off Cala del Faro at around 2 nautical miles holds dentex and grouper in 20 to 30 metres of water. The deeper drop-off south of La Concha mountain, about 5 nautical miles out, is a summer spot for amberjack and bonito. From Marbella Marina, the artificial reef built from concrete blocks at 3 nautical miles is a reliable bream and sea bass mark. The Río Verde estuary, just west of the marina, produces bass on the rising tide. Cabopino’s marine reserve is a no-fishing zone, but the edges just outside it hold red mullet and mackerel. Estepona gives you quick access to the Punta de la Doncella reef at 1.5 nautical miles, and the deeper water off the Guadalmina River mouth at 4 nautical miles for grouper. Sotogrande is the closest port to the Alboran Sea canyon, where the seabed drops to 200 metres within 6 nautical miles, a prime spot for swordfish and tuna. Benalmádena’s wrecks, including a sunken cargo ship at 8 nautical miles, hold snapper and barracuda. Your skipper will pick the best mark based on the wind and tide on the day.

What Gear Is Provided and What to Bring Yourself

The charter boat supplies everything you need to fish: medium-action spinning rods for light work, heavy trolling rods for tuna, and baitcasting reels for bottom fishing. Tackle includes hooks from size 2 to 10/0, swivels, leaders, and a mix of live bait like sardines or squid and artificial lures like poppers and soft plastics. The fishfinder shows the depth, bottom structure, and fish arches so the skipper can position you over the marks. You do not need to bring your own rods unless you have a favourite reel. What you should bring: sun cream with high SPF (the Mediterranean sun burns even through cloud), polarised sunglasses to see into the water, a light windbreaker for the morning breeze, and seasickness tablets if you are unsure about the motion. Most boats have a toilet, but check ahead if that matters. Leave the chunky jewellery and white trainers at home, they will smell of fish and sun cream by the end of the day.

How to Choose the Right Duration for Your Charter

Half-day charters (4 hours) work well for families with young children or for a quick introduction to fishing. You can cover one or two spots close to port and still have the afternoon free for the beach. Full-day charters (8 hours) let you run further offshore, fish multiple marks, and target bigger species like tuna or swordfish. They also include a lunch break, often at anchor in a sheltered bay. Some skippers offer a 6-hour option, which is a compromise if you want more time than a half-day but do not need a full day. For serious anglers targeting bluefin tuna, a full-day charter is the minimum because the fish move fast and you may need to relocate several times. For bottom fishing in the inshore reefs, a half-day is usually enough to fill the ice box with bream and sea bass. Talk to the BoatHire24 team when booking, they can recommend the best duration based on what you want to catch.

Typical Costs for Add-Ons and Extras

The base price for private boat hire Marbella fishing includes the essentials, but some extras cost more. A professional photographer to document the catch can add EUR 100 to EUR 200 for the day. If you want the skipper to arrange for a restaurant to cook your catch, expect a service fee of around EUR 20 to EUR 30 plus the restaurant bill. Some boats offer a barbecue on board for an extra EUR 50 to EUR 100, which includes the chef and ingredients. Pick-up from your hotel to the marina is sometimes included for luxury yachts, but for standard charters you pay for a taxi or drive yourself. Fuel surcharges are rare because fuel is included in the price, but if you ask for a long run to the Strait of Gibraltar, the skipper may add a fuel supplement of EUR 100 to EUR 200. Always confirm what is included before you pay the deposit. Read the terms on the BoatHire24 platform for each boat listing.

How Weather Affects Your Fishing Charter

The Costa del Sol has over 300 sunny days a year, so cancellations due to weather are uncommon. But the wind matters. The Levante wind blows from the east and can kick up a choppy sea, especially in the Strait of Gibraltar. The Poniente wind from the west is lighter and usually brings calm conditions. In summer, the thermal breeze picks up around midday and drops in the evening, so morning departures are generally calmer. In winter, the sea can be rough after a storm, but the fish often bite better in the unsettled conditions. Your skipper will check the forecast the day before and will cancel only if the wind exceeds 20 knots or the swell is over 2 metres. If the charter is cancelled due to weather, you get a full refund or a rescheduled date. For private boat hire Marbella fishing, the skipper wants you to catch fish, so they will not take you out if the conditions are dangerous or unproductive.

What Happens to the Fish You Catch

You keep what you catch within legal limits. The minimum sizes in Andalusia are: sea bass 36 cm, gilthead bream 20 cm, red mullet 11 cm, and bluefin tuna 30 kg (yes, that is a minimum weight of 30 kg for tuna). The skipper knows the regulations and will measure your catch. You can take the fish back to your accommodation and cook it yourself, or ask a local restaurant to prepare it. Some restaurants in Puerto Banús and Marbella old town will cook your catch for a corkage fee of around EUR 10 to EUR 15 per person. If you do not want the fish, the skipper will release it or keep it for their own use. Catch and release is encouraged for grouper and dentex because they are slow-growing. The skipper will show you how to handle the fish to minimise harm if you plan to release it. You can also ask for the fish to be filleted and vacuum-packed for transport, useful if you are flying home and want to take the catch as frozen luggage.

Related guides

  • Yacht Charter Fuengirola: Budget-Friendly Options
  • Boat Rental Estepona: Affordable Day Trips with Skipper
  • Catamaran Hire Marbella: Group Discounts & Private Charters
  • Motor Yacht Rental Marbella: Power Cruises with Skipper
  • Yacht Hire Marbella: Book Your Dream Boat

faqs.q: Do I need a fishing licence for private boat hire Marbella fishing?
faqs.a: No. The skipper holds a commercial fishing licence that covers everyone on board. You do not need to buy a separate licence or carry any paperwork.

faqs.q: Can I keep the fish I catch?
faqs.a: Yes, within legal size and bag limits. The skipper will clean and fillet your catch on the boat. You can take it back to your accommodation or ask a local restaurant to cook it for you.

faqs.q: What is the maximum number of people on a private fishing charter?
faqs.a: It depends on the boat. Small RIBs take up to 4 people. Motor yachts and catamarans can take 8 to 12. Check the boat listing on BoatHire24 for the maximum capacity.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad?
faqs.a: The skipper decides whether conditions are safe to sail. If the charter is cancelled due to weather, you get a full refund or the option to reschedule. If you cancel on the day because you do not like the forecast, the skipper may keep the deposit.

faqs.q: Can I bring my own fishing gear?
faqs.a: Yes, you can bring your own rods and reels if you prefer. The boat will still have backup gear and bait. Let the skipper know in advance so they can make space.

faqs.q: How far offshore do the fishing charters go?
faqs.a: Most inshore fishing is within 3 to 5 nautical miles of the coast. For game fish like tuna or swordfish, the skipper may run 10 to 15 nautical miles offshore. The boat type determines how far you can comfortably go.

faqs.q: Is private boat hire Marbella fishing suitable for beginners?
faqs.a: Yes. Skippers are used to teaching novices how to cast, reel, and handle the fish. They will set you up with simple tackle and show you the basics. Children are welcome on most boats.

title: Catamaran Rental Estepona Group: Book for 20
metaDescription: Book a catamaran rental in Estepona for your group of up to 20. All charters include a skipper. Compare prices, ports, and boat sizes. Reserve now with BoatHire24.
h1: Catamaran Rental Estepona Group: Book for 20
intro:
If you need a catamaran rental in Estepona for a group of up to 20, you are looking at the most practical way to spend a day on the Costa del Sol with friends or family. Catamarans are stable, spacious, and come with a licensed skipper, so you can focus on the sun, the sea, and the sangria. We are BoatHire24, and we list the best catamarans available from Estepona port and nearby marinas. No hidden fees, no fuss.

Here is how it works: you choose the date, the boat, and the optional extras (food, drinks, water toys). We handle the booking. The skipper takes care of navigation, safety, and finding the best spots for swimming, dolphin watching, or just drifting. You just show up with your group and the sunscreen.
bodyHtml:
What Is Included in a Catamaran Rental Estepona Group Charter

Every catamaran rental through BoatHire24 includes a licensed skipper, fuel for up to 4-6 hours of cruising, basic safety equipment, and VAT. Most boats also include a Bluetooth speaker, a cooler, and a shaded saloon area. What you do not get is a crew to serve you (unless you add catering) or a guaranteed itinerary. The skipper will suggest a route based on weather and your preferences, but you can ask to stop at Cala del Faro or cruise along the coast toward Puerto Banús.

For groups of 20, you will typically need a catamaran between 40 and 50 feet. These boats have two or three hulls, so they are much more stable than monohulls. That means less seasickness and more room to walk around. The deck space is usually enough for everyone to lounge, eat, and jump off the trampoline nets. Some catamarans have a flybridge, which gives you a higher viewpoint and extra seating.

Estepona Port: Your Departure Point

Estepona marina (Puerto Deportivo de Estepona) is a modern port with around 300 berths, restaurants, and a small supermarket. It is less crowded than Puerto Banús, which means quicker boarding and more relaxed vibes. The port is a 10-minute walk from Estepona old town, so you can grab breakfast or supplies before you set off. If you are driving, there is paid parking nearby.

From Estepona, you can head east toward Marbella and Puerto Banús (about 10 NM) or west toward the Strait of Gibraltar (about 20 NM to Tarifa). The water is generally calm in summer, with sea temperatures around 22-25 degrees C. The prevailing wind is from the east (Levante) or west (Poniente), so the skipper will pick a sheltered route if the wind picks up.

Boat Options for a Group of 20

We list several catamarans suitable for 20 passengers. Most are Lagoon, Fountaine Pajot, or Bali models, ranging from 40 to 50 feet. A typical example is a Lagoon 450, which sleeps 8-10 but can carry 20 for day trips. The price for a full-day charter (8 hours) starts from around EUR 1,500 to EUR 2,500, depending on the season, boat age, and included extras. Half-day charters (4 hours) start from around EUR 900 to EUR 1,500.

If your group is smaller than 20, you might consider a motor yacht or a sailing yacht instead. But for a group of 20, catamarans are the best option because of the space and stability. You can also book two smaller catamarans if you want to split the group, but that doubles the cost. Check our search page to filter by boat type and capacity.

Pricing Guidance for Catamaran Rental Estepona Group

Prices vary by season, boat size, and duration. In low season (November to March), a full-day charter for 20 might cost around EUR 1,200 to EUR 1,800. In high season (June to September), expect EUR 2,000 to EUR 3,000. Peak dates like Easter or the August bank holiday can push prices higher. Always confirm the exact price with the owner or operator before booking.

Extras like a barbecue lunch, open bar, paddleboards, or a snorkeling kit add EUR 15 to EUR 50 per person. Some operators include these in a package deal. If you want a sunset cruise (2-3 hours), prices are lower, around EUR 600 to EUR 1,000 for the boat. But for a group of 20, a full-day charter gives you more value per person.

Best Time of Year for a Group Catamaran Charter

The Costa del Sol has a Mediterranean climate with over 300 sunny days a year. The best months for a catamaran rental in Estepona are May to October, when the sea is warm and the wind is gentle. July and August are the hottest, with temperatures around 30-35 degrees C, so bring hats and plenty of water. Spring and autumn are cooler but still pleasant, with fewer crowds and lower prices.

Winter charters are possible but less common because the sea can be choppy and the air temperature drops to 15-18 degrees C. Some operators still run trips if the weather is calm. If you are flexible, you can find good deals in November or February. Just check the forecast and pack a jacket.

What to Expect on the Day of Your Charter

On the day, you will meet the skipper at the port about 15 minutes before departure. The skipper will give a safety briefing, show you the life jackets and toilet, and explain the itinerary. You can bring your own food and drinks (most operators allow it), or pre-order a catering package. The boat will have a fridge and a cooler, so bring ice packs for perishables.

Once you leave the marina, the skipper will head out to the open sea. Common stops include the area around the Punta de la Doncella (a rocky headland with clear water) or the coast near the Río Guadalmina. If the group wants to see dolphins, the skipper can head west toward the Strait of Gibraltar, where pods of common dolphins and pilot whales are often sighted. The trip takes about 30-40 minutes from Estepona.

Swimming stops are usually in sheltered coves like Cala del Faro or the beach at Playa del Cristo. The water is clear and calm, perfect for snorkeling or just floating. The skipper will anchor for 30-60 minutes depending on the schedule. You can also use paddleboards or a kayak if they are included.

How to Book a Catamaran Rental Estepona Group

Booking through BoatHire24 is straightforward. You can browse our listings, compare prices, and read the boat descriptions. Once you find a catamaran you like, click the link to check availability and send a booking request. The operator will confirm within 24 hours. You pay a deposit (usually 30-50%) to secure the date, and the balance is due on the day of the charter.

We recommend booking at least two weeks in advance, especially in high season. For last-minute bookings, you can try calling the operator directly, but availability is limited. If you need help choosing a boat, contact us and we can suggest options based on your group size and budget. Start your search on our BoatHire24 homepage.

Who Is This Charter For?

Catamaran rentals for groups of 20 are ideal for birthday parties, corporate events, bachelor or bachelorette groups, family reunions, or just a big group of friends who want to escape the beach crowds. The boat is private, so you control the music, the food, and the itinerary. It is also a good option for wedding parties or team-building days.

If you have children in the group, catamarans are safer than motorboats because they have less roll and more deck space. Most operators allow children of all ages, but check the life jacket sizes. For older guests or people with mobility issues, the boarding ramp and stable platform make it easier to get on and off. Just let the operator know in advance.

Alternatives to a Catamaran Rental Estepona Group

If a catamaran is not available or you want a different experience, consider a motor yacht for up to 20 guests. Motor yachts are faster (20-30 knots) and have more luxurious interiors, but they are less stable and have less deck space. Prices are similar to catamarans. Sailing yachts are also an option, but they are slower and have less room for large groups.

For smaller groups (up to 10), a RIB or speedboat is cheaper and more exciting, but not suitable for 20 people. If your group is exactly 20, a catamaran is the best balance of space, stability, and cost. You can also check our blog for more tips on choosing the right boat for your group.

Local Attractions Near Estepona

Estepona itself is worth a visit before or after your charter. The old town has whitewashed buildings, flower-filled balconies, and a lively square with tapas bars. The Orchidarium (a botanical garden with over 1,000 orchid species) is a popular stop. If you have time, drive to the nearby town of Casares, a hilltop village with stunning views of the coast.

From the water, you can see the Sierra Bermeja mountains behind Estepona, which are often covered in snow in winter. The coastline is dotted with small coves and cliffs, making it a scenic backdrop for photos. If you head east, you will pass the luxury developments of Marbella and the golden sands of the Costa del Sol. The skipper can point out landmarks like La Concha mountain and the Marbella Club Hotel.

Comparing Catamarans for 20: Lagoon vs Fountaine Pajot vs Bali

The three main brands you will see on BoatHire24 for groups of 20 are Lagoon, Fountaine Pajot, and Bali. Each has a different layout. Lagoon catamarans (like the 450 or 46) are known for their solid build and large flybridge, giving you a second seating area above the main deck. Fountaine Pajot models (such as the Lucia 40 or Saba 50) tend to have more open-plan saloons and larger trampoline nets at the bow, which is good for sunbathing. Bali catamarans (like the 4.2 or 4.6) have a unique open transom that extends the living space to the back of the boat, making it easier to jump in and out of the water. For a group of 20, the key difference is where people can sit. A flybridge adds about 6-8 extra seats, while a Bali's open stern can fit another 4-5 people on cushions. Check the photos and deck plan on each listing to see which layout suits your group.

Departure Ports Near Estepona for Your Group Charter

While Estepona is your main departure point, you can also consider departing from nearby ports if it is more convenient. Puerto Banús is about 10 NM east (20 minutes by boat) and has more restaurants and shops, but it is busier and parking is expensive. Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is 8 NM east and offers a similar vibe with slightly lower costs. Cabopino, 15 NM east, is smaller and quieter, with a sandy beach right next to the port. Sotogrande, 12 NM west, is a luxury residential marina with a golf course and upmarket restaurants. Benalmádena, 30 NM east, is a larger port with a marina promenade and an aquarium. If you are staying in Estepona, the local port is the best option because it is less crowded and you can walk from the old town. But if your group is based in Marbella or Sotogrande, you can book a catamaran that departs from there instead. Use the search page to filter by departure port.

Typical Itineraries for a Catamaran Rental Estepona Group of 20

A full-day charter from Estepona usually follows one of three routes. The east route takes you past the beaches of El Saladillo and Guadalmina, then to the cliffs of Cala del Faro, where you can anchor for swimming. The total distance is about 8-10 NM, so you have plenty of time for a long lunch stop. The west route heads toward the Strait of Gibraltar, passing the Dunas de Artola nature reserve and the town of Manilva. You might see dolphins here, especially between April and October. The third option is to stay closer to Estepona and visit the island of Isla de la Doncella, a small rocky outcrop with clear water and a small beach. The skipper will choose based on the wind and sea conditions. If you have a preference, tell the skipper at the start. For a half-day charter (4 hours), the east or west route is usually cut short to one swimming stop and a coastal cruise.

Food and Drink Options for Your Group Charter

Most catamaran operators in Estepona allow you to bring your own food and drinks. This is the cheapest option: you can buy supplies from the Mercadona or Carrefour near the port. A typical group of 20 might spend around EUR 100-200 on snacks, sandwiches, soft drinks, and beers. If you want a barbecue, some boats have a gas grill on the back deck. You need to bring the meat and marinades. Alternatively, you can order a catering package from the operator. Prices range from EUR 25 to EUR 50 per person for a menu that includes paella, grilled fish, salads, and dessert. Some operators also offer an open bar with beer, wine, and sangria for an extra EUR 15-25 per person. For a sunset cruise, a simple tapas platter and a bottle of cava work well. Check the listing details to see what is included, or ask the operator when you book.

Weather and Sea Conditions for Estepona Group Charters

Estepona sits on the western Costa del Sol, which means it gets less wind than areas further east like Málaga or Nerja. The prevailing wind is the Levante (from the east), which can blow at 15-25 knots in summer afternoons. The skipper will usually plan the route to stay in sheltered waters if the wind picks up. The Poniente (from the west) is lighter, around 10-15 knots, and brings calmer seas. In July and August, the sea is flat calm in the mornings, with a gentle breeze picking up around 2 PM. This is ideal for a group of 20 because the boat will not rock much. If you are prone to seasickness, take a motion sickness tablet an hour before departure, even on a catamaran. The water temperature in summer is 22-25 degrees C, so swimming is comfortable. In spring and autumn, the water is cooler (18-20 degrees C), and a wetsuit is optional but not necessary for a short dip.

Safety and Comfort Tips for a Large Group on a Catamaran

With 20 people on a 45-foot catamaran, space can feel tight if everyone crowds on one side. Spread out across the trampoline nets, the flybridge (if available), the saloon seating, and the stern platform. The skipper will tell you the maximum number of people allowed on the flybridge at once (usually 8-10). Do not sit on the guardrails or the bowsprit while the boat is moving. Life jackets are stored in the cockpit lockers; the skipper will show you where they are. If anyone in your group cannot swim, let the skipper know so they can provide a buoyancy aid. The toilet on a catamaran is a marine head with a pump; do not flush anything other than toilet paper. Bring a towel and a change of clothes if you plan to swim, because the deck can get slippery. Sun cream is essential, even on cloudy days, because the reflection off the water amplifies UV rays.

Booking Terms and Cancellation Policies for Estepona Catamarans

Each operator on BoatHire24 has their own booking terms, but most follow a standard pattern. You pay a non-refundable deposit of 30-50% to secure the date. The balance is due on the day of the charter, usually in cash or by card. If you cancel more than 14 days before the charter, you might get a partial refund (less the deposit). Cancellations within 7 days are usually non-refundable. If the operator cancels due to bad weather or mechanical issues, you get a full refund or the option to reschedule. Check the cancellation policy on the listing page before you book. For a group of 20, it is worth taking out travel insurance that covers charter cancellations, especially if you are travelling from abroad. Read our blog for a detailed guide on what to look for in a charter contract.

Combining a Catamaran Charter with Other Activities in Estepona

If you are spending a few days in Estepona, you can combine a catamaran charter with other local attractions. The Estepona Orchidarium is a 15-minute walk from the port and costs around EUR 5 per person. The Selwo Aventura wildlife park, 10 minutes by car, has zip lines and animal encounters (around EUR 29 per adult). For a more relaxed day, the old town has a market on Sunday mornings with local crafts and food. If your group is into hiking, the Sierra Bermeja mountains offer trails with views of the coast and the Strait of Gibraltar. The walk to the summit of Los Reales (1,449 metres) takes about 3 hours. You can also book a wine tasting at the Bodega Cortijo Los Aguilares, 15 minutes from Estepona, which produces organic reds and whites. For a post-charter dinner, try the restaurant El Campanario on the port, which serves grilled fish and paella for around EUR 30-40 per person.

What to Wear and Bring for a Catamaran Rental Estepona Group Charter

For a day on a catamaran in Estepona, wear lightweight, quick-dry clothing. Swimwear under a t-shirt or cover-up is standard. Non-slip shoes (boat shoes or barefoot) are best on deck; avoid high heels or black-soled shoes that can mark the fibreglass. Bring a hat with a brim, sunglasses with a strap, and a high-SPF sun cream. A light jacket or windbreaker is useful for the afternoon breeze, especially in spring or autumn. For swimming, pack a towel, snorkel mask, and fins if you have them. Some catamarans provide snorkelling gear, but check the listing. A dry bag for your phone and wallet is a good idea because the deck can get wet. If you plan to use the Bluetooth speaker, bring a playlist ready. The boat will have a 12V USB charger for phones, but bring a power bank if you need more juice. Leave valuables like jewellery or expensive cameras at home; the skipper is not responsible for lost items.

Why Estepona Is a Better Choice Than Puerto Banús for a Group of 20

Puerto Banús is famous for its superyachts and luxury shops, but for a group of 20 on a catamaran, Estepona has several advantages. The port is quieter, so boarding is faster and you do not have to queue with tourists. Parking is cheaper and easier: a public car park near Estepona port costs around EUR 10-15 for the day, compared to EUR 30-40 in Puerto Banús. The old town of Estepona is more authentic and less crowded, with tapas bars that charge EUR 3-5 for a plate, not EUR 10-15. On the water, the coastline near Estepona has more natural coves and fewer developments, so you feel further away from the crowds. The water quality is excellent, with Blue Flag beaches like Playa del Cristo and Playa de la Rada. If you want a more relaxed, private experience with your group, Estepona is the better choice. For a night out after the charter, you can take a taxi to Puerto Banús in 20 minutes, but you will appreciate the calm of Estepona after a day on the water.

Related guides

  • Boat Rental Puerto Banus: Wide Fleet with Skipper
  • Catamaran Rental Estepona: Group Charters with Skipper
  • Private Yacht Hire Puerto Banus: Exclusive Charters

faqs.q: Can I bring my own food and drinks on a catamaran rental in Estepona?
faqs.a: Yes, most operators allow you to bring your own food and drinks. Some have a corkage fee if you bring alcohol, but many include a cooler and ice. Check with the specific boat owner when booking.

faqs.q: How far in advance should I book a catamaran for a group of 20 in Estepona?
faqs.a: We recommend booking at least two weeks in advance, especially in high season (June to September). Last-minute bookings are possible but limited. For peak dates like Easter or August, book a month ahead.

faqs.q: Is a skipper included in the catamaran rental price?
faqs.a: Yes, every catamaran rental through BoatHire24 includes a licensed skipper. You do not need a boating license. The skipper handles navigation, safety, and anchoring. You just relax and enjoy.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad on the day of my charter?
faqs.a: The skipper will assess the conditions and may delay, reroute, or cancel the trip for safety. If the charter is cancelled by the operator, you get a full refund or can reschedule. Bad weather is rare in summer on the Costa del Sol.

faqs.q: Can we swim and snorkel during the charter?
faqs.a: Yes, swimming stops are standard. The skipper will anchor in a calm cove or near a beach. Snorkeling gear is often included or available for a small extra fee. Bring your own mask if you prefer.

faqs.q: Are there toilets on catamarans for 20 guests?
faqs.a: Yes, most catamarans have one or two toilets (heads) and a small shower. For a group of 20, you may have to queue, but it is manageable. Some larger catamarans have more facilities.

faqs.q: What is the cancellation policy for a group catamaran rental?
faqs.a: Cancellation policies vary by operator. Typically, you can cancel up to 7 days before for a full refund of the deposit. Within 7 days, the deposit may be non-refundable. Check the terms when you book.

title: Motor Yacht Charter Estepona Day: Coastal Cruise
metaDescription: Book a motor yacht charter Estepona day trip for a coastal cruise. Compare boats from EUR 1,200, includes skipper, fuel, and lunch. Reserve online now.
h1: Motor Yacht Charter Estepona Day: Coastal Cruise
intro:
You want a day on the water around Estepona, but you don't want to deal with paperwork, fuel costs, or navigating yourself. A motor yacht charter Estepona day trip is exactly that: a private yacht with a licensed skipper, fuel included, lunch on board, and a route that hugs the Costa del Sol coast from Estepona port past the Sierra Bermeja foothills. No surprises, no hidden fees, just a boat that leaves at 10am and returns by 6pm.

BoatHire24 lists available motor yachts for day charters departing from Estepona Marina (Puerto Deportivo de Estepona). You pick the size, the number of guests, and the extras, jet ski, paddleboard, or just a cooler full of drinks. The skipper handles the navigation, anchoring, and any questions you have about the coastline. You just show up, step on board, and decide whether to sunbathe on the foredeck or swim in a quiet cove.
bodyHtml:
What a Motor Yacht Charter Estepona Day Includes

Every motor yacht charter Estepona day booking through BoatHire24 comes with a few standard items that remove the typical rental headaches. First, a licensed skipper is included in the price. That means you don't need a boating licence, you don't need to speak Spanish, and you don't need to worry about anchoring or docking. The skipper knows the local waters, the wind patterns, and the best spots to stop for a swim.

Fuel is included for up to 8 hours of cruising. Most day charters cover around 30 to 40 nautical miles (NM) at cruising speed, which is enough to reach the Marbella coast or head south towards the Strait of Gibraltar. Lunch is served on board, usually a selection of Spanish cold meats, cheese, bread, salad, and a bottle of white wine or cava. You can upgrade to a full barbecue or paella for an extra EUR 15 to EUR 25 per person.

Typical extras include a stand-up paddleboard (EUR 50), a two-person jet ski towed behind the yacht (EUR 150), or a professional photographer for the day (EUR 200). Snorkelling gear is usually free. You can also bring your own music via Bluetooth speaker, though most yachts have a built-in sound system.

Estepona Marina: Your Departure Point

Estepona Marina, officially Puerto Deportivo de Estepona, sits at the western end of the Costa del Sol, about 15 minutes by car from Puerto Banús. The marina has 400 berths, a handful of chiringuitos (beach bars), and a fuel dock. For a motor yacht charter Estepona day, you meet the crew at the marina office or directly at the pontoon. Parking is available in the underground car park next to the marina, costing around EUR 2 per hour.

The advantage of departing from Estepona is that you avoid the congestion of Puerto Banús. The channel is wide, the sea state is usually calm, and you can be cruising at 20 knots within five minutes of leaving the harbour. The skipper will give a quick safety briefing while you cast off, then you head west or east depending on the wind and your preference.

If you want to combine the charter with a meal on land, the marina has several restaurants serving grilled fish and paella. Your crew can drop you back at the marina by 5pm, leaving time for a walk along the promenade before dinner.

Boat Options for a Day Charter

The motor yachts available for a motor yacht charter Estepona day range from 30 feet (9 metres) to 60 feet (18 metres). Smaller boats, like a Jeanneau 35 or a Beneteau Flyer 36, cost from around EUR 1,200 for a full day and take up to 8 guests. These are sporty, open-plan designs with a small cabin, a galley, and a sun pad at the bow.

Mid-range yachts, 40 to 50 feet (12 to 15 metres), such as a Sunseeker Portofino 46 or a Princess V48, cost from EUR 2,500 to EUR 4,000 per day. They have a flybridge with seating, a full kitchen, a spacious saloon, and two or three cabins below deck. These yachts can handle up to 10 or 12 guests comfortably.

Larger yachts, 55 to 60 feet (17 to 18 metres), like an Azimut 60 or a Ferretti 55, cost from EUR 5,000 per day and accommodate up to 12 guests. They offer more interior space, a sun deck with a bar, and a larger swim platform. If you want a superyacht (over 24 metres), price is on request and typically starts at EUR 10,000 per day.

Browse all available options on the search page to filter by size, price, and departure port.

Coastal Cruise Route: What to Expect

A typical motor yacht charter Estepona day cruise follows one of two routes. The western route heads towards the Strait of Gibraltar, passing the Punta de la Doncella lighthouse and the sandy beaches of El Cristo and La Rada. About 45 minutes from Estepona, you reach the fishing village of Guadiaro, where the river meets the sea. The water here is often calm, making it a good spot for anchoring and swimming.

The eastern route takes you past the cliffs of Cala del Faro and towards Marbella. You can stop at the small cove of Cala de la Sardina, which is sheltered from the wind and has clear water. From there, you can see the coastline all the way to Puerto Banús and the La Concha mountain in the background. The skipper will anchor for an hour or two, and you can use the paddleboard or snorkel around the rocks.

If you want a longer trip, you can request a route that goes all the way to Gibraltar, where you might spot dolphins or even whales. That adds about two hours of cruising each way, so you would need to start earlier or extend the charter to 10 hours. The skipper will adjust the itinerary based on the weather and your preferences.

Pricing Guidance for a Day Charter

Prices for a motor yacht charter Estepona day vary by boat size, season, and extras. The table below gives a rough guide based on typical listings on BoatHire24. Exact prices depend on the specific yacht and availability.

Boat LengthGuest CapacityPrice Range (EUR per day)
30-35 ft6-81,200 - 1,800
40-50 ft10-122,500 - 4,000
55-60 ft125,000 - 7,000
Superyacht (24m+)12+Price on request

All prices include skipper, fuel, lunch, water, and soft drinks. Extras like jet ski, photographer, or premium catering are additional. You pay a 50% deposit at booking and the balance 7 days before departure. Cancellations up to 14 days before are fully refundable, minus a 5% processing fee.

For a full breakdown of costs and what's included, read the BoatHire24 blog guide on charter pricing.

Best Season for a Motor Yacht Charter in Estepona

The best months for a motor yacht charter Estepona day are May through October. Sea temperatures range from 18°C in May to 26°C in August. Air temperatures hover around 25°C to 32°C, with light winds from the east (levante) or west (poniente). July and August are the busiest, so book at least 2 to 3 weeks in advance.

Spring (April to May) and autumn (September to October) offer quieter conditions, fewer boats on the water, and lower prices by about 15 to 20 percent. The water is cooler in April (around 16°C), but the sun is still warm. If you want to avoid crowds and don't mind a wetsuit for swimming, these shoulder months are a good choice.

Winter charters are possible but less common. The sea can be choppy, and the wind is stronger. Most motor yachts remain in the water, but the skipper may limit the route to sheltered areas. Prices drop by 30 to 40 percent from November to March.

How to Book a Motor Yacht Charter Estepona Day

Booking a motor yacht charter Estepona day on BoatHire24 takes about five minutes. Go to the BoatHire24 homepage, select Estepona as the departure port, choose the date, and filter by boat type (motor yacht). You will see a list of available yachts with photos, specifications, and the total price.

Click on a listing to see the full itinerary, included amenities, and any optional extras. You can send a direct message to the owner to ask about specific requirements, such as dietary restrictions or a custom route. Once you confirm, you pay a 50% deposit via credit card or bank transfer. The owner sends a confirmation email with the meeting point and the skipper's contact details.

On the day, arrive at the marina 15 minutes before departure. The skipper will greet you at the pontoon, show you the safety equipment, and answer any last-minute questions. Then you cast off and enjoy the cruise.

Who Is This Charter For

A motor yacht charter Estepona day works for couples, families with older children, or groups of friends. It is not ideal for toddlers or infants because the deck has no railings and the boat moves at speed. Most yachts accept children aged 5 and above, but check with the owner if you have younger kids.

If you are a solo traveller, you can book a private charter, but you pay the full boat price. There are no shared or group charters on BoatHire24. For a smaller group or a tighter budget, consider a RIB speedboat or a licence-free day boat, which cost around EUR 400 to EUR 800 per day.

If you want a party atmosphere, choose a larger yacht with a flybridge and a powerful sound system. If you prefer a quiet, relaxing day, go for a smaller, more intimate boat. The skipper will match the vibe to your group.

Local Highlights Near Estepona

Estepona old town is worth a visit before or after your charter. It has narrow cobbled streets, whitewashed buildings, and flower-filled balconies. The Plaza de las Flores market sells local produce and crafts. If you have time, walk up to the Castillo de San Luis castle ruins for a view of the coast.

For a pre-charter breakfast, try a churro stand at the marina or a café on the Avenida de España. For a post-charter dinner, the harbour area has several seafood restaurants serving grilled sardines and fried anchovies. Book a table in advance during summer.

If you want to extend your stay, the area around Estepona has several golf courses, including the Finca Cortesín and the Estepona Golf Club. The beaches are clean and have Blue Flag status, but they get crowded in August. The charter gives you a private spot on the water away from the crowds.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why Estepona Beats Puerto Banús for a Day Charter

Puerto Banús gets all the attention, but for a motor yacht charter Estepona day, you get a better deal. Banús is a show-off harbour with superyachts and paparazzi. Estepona is a working marina with real fishermen, lower berth fees, and less ego. The skipper can leave the harbour without queuing behind a 50-metre Lürssen. The water is cleaner too, because you are further from the Guadalmina river runoff. If you want to see the Banús glamour, the skipper can cruise past it on the way to Marbella. You get the views without the parking hassle.

The marina staff in Estepona are more relaxed about boarding times. If you arrive 30 minutes early, you can sit at the beach bar with a coffee and watch the boats come in. In Banús, you pay EUR 5 for the same coffee and get a side of attitude. For a family or a group of friends who want a stress-free start, Estepona wins every time.

Fuel Costs and Range: What You Actually Burn

Fuel is included in the price of a motor yacht charter Estepona day, but it helps to understand what that covers. A 40-foot motor yacht with twin diesel engines burns about 80 to 120 litres per hour at cruising speed (20 to 25 knots). At EUR 1.50 per litre, that is EUR 120 to EUR 180 per hour. Over 8 hours, the fuel bill alone is EUR 960 to EUR 1,440. The charter price absorbs that, so you are not watching the gauge all day.

If you ask the skipper to open the throttles to 30 knots for a thrill run, fuel consumption jumps to 200 litres per hour. Most skippers will do this for 10 or 15 minutes, then drop back to a comfortable cruise. The range at cruising speed is about 150 to 200 NM, which is enough to reach Gibraltar and back with a margin. If you want to go to the Nerja caves or the Moroccan coast, you need a yacht with larger fuel tanks, which costs more. For a standard day, the included fuel is more than enough.

Weather Windows and the Levante Wind

The Costa del Sol has two main winds. The levante comes from the east, blowing warm and dry air from the Sahara. It can kick up a chop in the afternoon, especially between Marbella and Estepona. The poniente comes from the west, bringing cooler, more humid air and flatter seas. For a motor yacht charter Estepona day, the skipper checks the forecast 24 hours before departure. If the levante is forecast above 20 knots, the skipper will suggest a route that stays close to the coast, where the sea is calmer.

In July and August, the thermal wind builds around 2pm and dies by sunset. That means the morning is glassy, the afternoon is bumpy, and the evening is calm again. If your group gets seasick, ask for a 10am to 6pm charter instead of noon to 8pm. You get the flat water in the morning for the swim stop, and the wind picks up just as you head back to port. The skipper will adjust the speed to keep the ride comfortable.

What to Bring on a Day Charter

Pack light for a motor yacht charter Estepona day. You need a swimsuit, a towel, sunscreen (factor 50, not the cheap stuff), sunglasses with a strap, and a light jacket or windbreaker. The sun reflects off the water and burns faster than on land. A hat is essential unless you want a red scalp. Leave the high heels at home; the deck gets slippery, and the crew will ask you to take them off anyway.

Bring a waterproof phone case or a dry bag for your valuables. The yacht has a small safe in the cabin, but it is not big enough for a laptop. If you want to take photos, a GoPro on a stick is better than a DSLR because of the spray. The skipper has a first aid kit and a fire extinguisher, but if you need specific medication, bring it yourself. The nearest pharmacy is at the marina, but it closes for siesta from 2pm to 5pm.

How to Tip the Crew on a Day Charter

Tipping is not mandatory on a motor yacht charter Estepona day, but it is appreciated. The crew works a 10-hour day including cleaning before and after the charter. A typical tip is 10 to 15 percent of the charter price, split between the skipper and the deckhand. For a EUR 3,000 charter, that is EUR 300 to EUR 450. If the service was average, EUR 100 is fine. If the skipper went out of the way to find dolphins or let you steer the boat, tip on the higher end.

Cash in euros is easiest. The crew does not carry a card machine. Some guests tip via bank transfer after the charter, but cash is more direct. Do not tip in dollars or pounds; the crew has to exchange it and loses on the rate. If you are unsure, ask the owner during booking what the crew expects. Most owners will say "not required but welcome", which means yes.

Alternative Departure Ports Near Estepona

If Estepona Marina is fully booked or you want a different starting point, you can book a motor yacht charter Estepona day from Sotogrande, about 15 minutes west by road. Sotogrande is a private marina with a polo club and gated communities. The boats there are generally larger and more expensive, starting at EUR 3,000 per day. The advantage is that you are closer to the Strait of Gibraltar, so dolphin sightings are almost guaranteed.

To the east, Cabopino Marina is a small, quiet harbour near the Artola sand dunes. It has a relaxed vibe and a good beach bar. The boats there are mostly 30 to 40 feet, costing from EUR 1,000 per day. Cabopino is closer to Marbella, so you can cruise to Puerto Banús in 20 minutes. Benalmádena is further east, about 40 minutes from Estepona by car, and has a large selection of motor yachts. All these ports are listed on the search page with filters for departure location and boat size.

What Happens in Bad Weather

If the weather turns bad on the day of your motor yacht charter Estepona day, the skipper has the final say on whether to sail. The decision is based on wind speed, wave height, and visibility. If the skipper cancels, you get a full refund or a free reschedule. If you decide to cancel because of a light drizzle, you lose the deposit. The definition of "bad weather" is subjective, but the skipper will not go out if the waves are over 1.5 metres or the wind is above 25 knots.

In practice, cancellations are rare on the Costa del Sol. The region gets over 300 days of sunshine per year. If a storm is forecast, the owner will contact you 48 hours before to discuss options. You can move the charter to the next day or switch to a different port where the weather is better. The crew would rather lose a day of income than risk the boat and the guests.

Combining the Charter with a Land Excursion

A motor yacht charter Estepona day usually runs from 10am to 6pm. That leaves the evening free for a land activity. Estepona old town is a 10-minute walk from the marina. The streets are quiet after 8pm when the shops close. The Plaza de las Flores has a few tapas bars where you can eat for EUR 15 to EUR 20 per person. For a fancier meal, try the Restaurante El Puerto on the marina front, which serves grilled sea bass and lobster.

If you have a car, drive 20 minutes to the village of Casares, a whitewashed hill town with a Moorish castle. The views from the top stretch to Gibraltar on a clear day. Alternatively, the Cueva del Gato (Cat's Cave) is a natural swimming hole about 30 minutes inland. The water is cold but crystal clear. These trips work well the day before or after the charter, not on the same day, because you will be tired from the sun and the sea.

Related guides
