faqs.q: How many people can fit on a private yacht for a hen party?
faqs.a: Most motor yachts and catamarans on BoatHire24 accommodate 10 to 16 passengers. Smaller RIBs take 6 to 8. Check the boat listing for the maximum capacity, which includes the skipper. Do not exceed the limit because insurance and safety regulations apply.

faqs.q: Can we bring our own alcohol and food on the yacht?
faqs.a: Yes, you can bring your own drinks and food. Most boats have a fridge and a cooler. Some owners charge a corkage fee for glass bottles. Avoid red wine on white upholstery. You can also order a catering package through the boat owner for an extra cost.

faqs.q: Do we need to tip the skipper?
faqs.a: Tipping is not mandatory but is appreciated. A typical tip is 10 to 15 per cent of the charter price, given in cash at the end of the trip. The skipper works for the full duration and handles all the logistics, so a tip recognises good service.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad on the day?
faqs.a: The skipper decides if it is safe to sail. If the charter is cancelled due to weather, you get a full refund or can reschedule. If you cancel for personal reasons, the cancellation policy of the specific boat applies, usually a partial refund up to 48 hours before.

faqs.q: Can we play our own music on the boat?
faqs.a: Most boats have a Bluetooth speaker or an auxiliary input. You can connect your phone and play your playlist. Keep the volume reasonable so the skipper can hear the radio and other vessels. Some owners provide a premium sound system.

faqs.q: Is swimming allowed during the charter?
faqs.a: Yes, the skipper will anchor in a calm bay where you can swim. The boat usually has a swim ladder. Bring towels and sunscreen. The sea temperature from June to October is 20 to 25 degrees C, comfortable for swimming.

faqs.q: How far in advance should we book a hen party yacht?
faqs.a: For summer months (June to September), book at least two to three weeks ahead. For larger groups or specific boats, book a month in advance. In spring and autumn, one week is usually enough. Last-minute bookings are possible but have limited availability.

title: Fishing Charter Estepona Half Day: Morning Catch
metaDescription: Book a half-day fishing charter in Estepona. Morning trips from EUR 350 for up to 6 people. Rods, bait, and skipper included. Catch sea bass, bream, and more.
h1: Fishing Charter Estepona Half Day: Morning Catch
intro:
You want to get out on the water, drop a line, and land something worth photographing before lunch. A half-day fishing charter in Estepona is the straightforward answer. You leave the marina at first light, motor out to the grounds off the Costa del Sol, and spend four to five hours chasing sea bass, bream, mackerel, and the occasional dentex. The skipper handles the navigation, the rods are rigged, and the bait is ready. You just turn up with sunglasses and a sense of patience.

Estepona is a solid choice for a half-day trip because the fishing grounds are close to the harbour. You are not wasting two hours getting to the spot. The marina itself is quieter than Puerto Banús, which means less traffic and more time with your line in the water. Most charters run from 7am to midday, which avoids the afternoon heat and the afternoon chop. If you are staying in Marbella, Sotogrande, or even Gibraltar, Estepona is a 20 to 40 minute drive. It makes for an easy morning.
bodyHtml:
What You Get on a Half-Day Fishing Charter in Estepona

A half-day charter typically runs four to five hours. The boat is a purpose-built fishing vessel, usually 6 to 8 metres, with a outboard or inboard diesel engine. You get a licensed skipper who knows the local marks. He brings the rods, reels, tackle, bait, and ice for your catch. You do not need a fishing licence because the charter company holds the commercial permit. Everything is included except your lunch, though most trips offer a small cooler with water and soft drinks. If you want beer, bring your own.

The skipper will work the drift or anchor over rocky bottoms and sandy patches. The main target species are European sea bass (lubina), gilthead seabream (dorada), common pandora (besugo), mackerel (caballa), and horse mackerel (jurel). In spring and autumn, you might hook a dentex or a greater amberjack. The skipper will fillet your catch at the end of the trip, so you can take it back to your holiday apartment or to a local restaurant that will cook it for you.

Most boats take a maximum of six passengers. If you are a solo angler or a couple, you can join a shared charter, but the norm is private hire. Expect to pay from around EUR 350 for a private half-day trip for up to four people. Larger boats for six people run closer to EUR 500. These are guide prices. Actual costs depend on the boat size, the season, and the specific operator. You can check current availability on BoatHire24.

Why Pick Estepona for a Half-Day Fishing Trip

Estepona sits at the western end of the Costa del Sol, just east of the Gibraltar Strait. The coastline here is less developed than Marbella, and the fishing grounds are closer to shore. You can be on a productive mark within 15 to 20 minutes of leaving the marina. That is the main advantage over ports like Benalmádena or Málaga, where you might need a longer run to find clean water.

The Estepona marine protected area, known as the Reserva Marítima de Estepona, runs along the coast from the harbour to the Punta de la Doncella. Inside the reserve, fishing is restricted, but the areas just outside it hold good populations of bream and bass. The skipper knows the boundaries. You will fish the edges of the reserve and the rocky reefs around the Cabo de Estepona. The water depth ranges from 10 to 40 metres, which is ideal for light tackle and bottom fishing.

Estepona old town is also worth a mention. After your trip, you can walk the cobbled streets, have a coffee in the Plaza de las Flores, or find a chiringuito on the beach for grilled fish. It is a civilised way to spend the rest of the morning.

The Boats: What to Expect on a Half-Day Charter

The typical half-day fishing boat in Estepona is a 6 to 8 metre RIB or a fibreglass centre console. These boats are stable enough for the coastal conditions but nimble enough to chase the fish. They have a small cabin or a centre console with a windscreen, so you get some shelter if the wind picks up. The deck space is open, which makes casting and fighting fish easier.

Engines are usually 150 to 250 hp outboards or a single inboard diesel. Speed is not the priority, but you will cruise at 15 to 20 knots to reach the grounds. Fuel is included in the charter price on most trips. If it is not, the operator will tell you upfront. Ask before you book.

Some boats are equipped with fishfinders and GPS plotters. The skipper uses these to locate wrecks, reefs, and bait balls. Do not expect a luxury yacht. This is a working fishing boat. It is clean and functional. If you want a luxury fishing experience with a sun deck and a galley, you need a larger sportfisher, and that will cost more. For a half-day trip, the simple boats work fine.

You can browse the available boats and compare prices on the BoatHire24 search page.

Pricing Guidance for Half-Day Fishing Charters in Estepona

Prices for a half-day fishing charter in Estepona start at around EUR 350 for a private trip for up to four people. For a boat that takes six passengers, expect EUR 450 to EUR 550. Shared charters, where you join a group of strangers, are less common but can be found for around EUR 80 to EUR 120 per person. Shared trips usually require a minimum of four anglers to run.

The price includes the boat, skipper, fuel, rods, reels, tackle, bait, and basic refreshments. It does not include tips, restaurant meals, or transport to the marina. Some operators offer a pickup service from your hotel for an extra fee. Ask when you book.

Deposits are typically 30 to 50 percent of the total, with the balance paid on the day. Cancellation policies vary. Most operators give a full refund if you cancel 48 hours in advance. Weather cancellations are free. If the skipper calls off the trip due to high winds or rough seas, you get your deposit back or you can reschedule.

For exact pricing on specific boats, see the listings on BoatHire24.

Best Season for Half-Day Fishing in Estepona

The fishing in Estepona is good year-round, but the species change with the seasons. Spring (March to May) brings sea bass and bream into the shallower water. The water temperature rises from 15°C to 18°C, and the fish become more active. Summer (June to September) is the peak season for mackerel and horse mackerel. The water hits 22°C to 24°C. You will also see bonito and small tuna in August and September. Autumn (October to November) is the best time for dentex and amberjack. The water cools, and the bigger predators come closer to shore. Winter (December to February) is quiet but productive for sea bass if you are willing to brave cooler temperatures. The water drops to 14°C, and the fish are deeper.

Morning trips in summer are preferable because the sea breeze picks up around midday. In winter, the mornings are calmer, and the fish feed more consistently in the low light. The skipper will adjust the timing based on the season. Most half-day trips depart at 7am or 8am and return by midday or 1pm.

How to Book a Half-Day Fishing Charter in Estepona

Booking is straightforward. You choose a date, pick a boat that fits your group size, and pay a deposit. On the day, you meet the skipper at the Estepona marina (Puerto Deportivo de Estepona) at the agreed time. The marina is at the western end of the promenade, near the old town. There is free parking nearby, but it fills up in summer. Arrive 15 minutes early.

What to bring: sunscreen, sunglasses, a hat, a light jacket or windbreaker, and any food or drinks you want. The boat has a small cooler, but it is not a full kitchen. Wear non-slip shoes. No black soles. They mark the deck. The skipper will provide life jackets if needed.

If you are a beginner, tell the skipper when you book. He will show you the basics: how to bait the hook, how to cast, and how to feel a bite. The rods are set up for bottom fishing, so the technique is simple. You drop the weight to the bottom, reel up a turn, and wait. When the rod tip twitches, you lift.

You can book directly on BoatHire24. The platform shows real-time availability and allows you to compare boats and prices. If you have questions, the support team can help you choose the right trip.

Who Should Book a Half-Day Fishing Charter in Estepona

This trip is for anyone who wants a taste of sea fishing without committing to a full day. It suits families with children aged eight and up, couples looking for a different holiday activity, and groups of friends who want a relaxed morning on the water. Experienced anglers will find the fishing decent, especially in spring and autumn. The half-day format is also good for corporate groups or team-building events because it is short enough to fit around a lunch or a meeting.

If you are prone to seasickness, the half-day trip is a safer bet than a full day. The boat stays close to shore, and the morning conditions are usually calm. Take a motion sickness tablet an hour before departure if you are unsure.

If you want a longer trip, a full-day charter (eight hours) allows you to reach the Gibraltar Strait or the deeper waters off Marbella. But for a quick, productive session, the half-day is the better option.

Other Fishing Options on the Costa del Sol

Estepona is not the only port offering half-day fishing. You can also find trips from Puerto Banús, Marbella Marina, Cabopino, Sotogrande, and Benalmádena. Each port has its own character and fishing grounds. Puerto Banús is busier but offers larger sportfishers. Sotogrande is quieter and closer to the Gibraltar Strait, which is good for tuna and bonito in summer. Benalmádena has a wide range of boats, from small RIBs to 12-metre cruisers. If you are based in Marbella, the Marbella Marina is the most convenient, but the fishing grounds are a bit farther out.

For a broader view of what is available, check the BoatHire24 blog, which covers the best fishing spots and seasons along the coast.

Fishing Charters vs Licence-Free Boat Hire

If you have a boat licence, you can rent a small boat without a skipper and fish on your own. Licence-free day boats are also available for rent without a licence, but they are limited to small outboard engines and restricted to inshore waters. For serious fishing, a charter with a skipper is better because he knows the marks and has the right gear. The cost difference is not huge. A licence-free boat for half a day costs around EUR 200 to EUR 300 for four people. A fishing charter costs EUR 350 to EUR 500. The extra money buys you expertise, better tackle, and a higher chance of catching fish.

If you are a competent angler with your own gear, you might prefer the independence of a self-drive boat. But for most visitors, the charter is the simpler and more productive option.

Final Considerations for Your Half-Day Fishing Trip

Book in advance during July and August. Charters sell out weeks ahead. For spring and autumn, you can often book a few days before. If the weather looks marginal, the skipper will contact you the evening before to confirm. Do not be disappointed if the trip is cancelled. Safety comes first. The wind in the Strait of Gibraltar can pick up quickly, and the sea state can change in an hour.

Keep your expectations realistic. Fishing is not a guarantee. You might catch a dozen mackerel, or you might catch one bream. The skipper will work to put you on the fish, but sometimes they are not biting. The experience of being on the water, watching the sunrise over La Concha, and seeing the coastline from the sea is worth the trip regardless.

If you do land a good catch, the local chiringuitos on the Estepona beachfront will often cook it for you for a small fee. Ask the skipper for a recommendation. It is a nice way to end the morning.

For bookings and more information, visit BoatHire24.

  • Marbella vs Puerto Banús: Which Departure Port to Choose
  • How Much Does a Yacht Charter in Marbella Cost?
  • Dolphin Watching Boat Trips from Marbella: A Full Guide

faqs.q: What is included in a half-day fishing charter in Estepona?
faqs.a: The charter includes the boat, a licensed skipper, fuel, rods, reels, tackle, bait, and basic refreshments such as water and soft drinks. The skipper will fillet your catch. You do not need a fishing licence.

faqs.q: How much does a half-day fishing charter in Estepona cost?
faqs.a: Prices start at around EUR 350 for a private trip for up to four people. For a boat that takes six passengers, expect EUR 450 to EUR 550. Shared charters cost around EUR 80 to EUR 120 per person.

faqs.q: What fish can I catch on a half-day trip in Estepona?
faqs.a: Common species include sea bass, gilthead seabream, common pandora, mackerel, and horse mackerel. In spring and autumn, you might catch dentex or greater amberjack. The skipper targets the best marks for the season.

faqs.q: Do I need a fishing licence for a half-day charter?
faqs.a: No. The charter company holds a commercial fishing permit that covers all passengers. You do not need to bring or buy a licence.

faqs.q: What time does a half-day fishing charter in Estepona start?
faqs.a: Most half-day trips depart at 7am or 8am and return by midday or 1pm. The exact time depends on the season and the skipper. Summer trips often start earlier to avoid the heat.

faqs.q: Can I bring my own fishing gear?
faqs.a: Yes, you can bring your own rods and reels if you prefer. The charter provides all necessary gear, so it is not required. If you bring your own, let the skipper know in advance.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad?
faqs.a: The skipper will cancel the trip if conditions are unsafe. You will receive a full refund of your deposit or the option to reschedule. Weather cancellations are free and common in winter.

title: Speedboat Hire Estepona Port No Licence: Easy Rental
metaDescription: Speedboat hire Estepona port no licence. Rent a boat without a licence from Estepona port. Instant online booking. Prices from around EUR 250.
h1: Speedboat Hire Estepona Port No Licence
keyword: speedboat hire Estepona port no licence
intro:
You can hire a speedboat from Estepona port without a licence. That is the short version. No paperwork, no exams, no Spanish official qualifications. Just turn up, get a 15-minute briefing, and take the wheel of a boat that will do 30 knots if you want it to. BoatHire24 lists the boats that are legal to drive without a licence from Estepona, and every one includes a licensed skipper as standard anyway, so the whole licence question is mostly irrelevant. But we get it: you want the freedom to drive yourself. The boats that allow that are called licence-free day boats, and they are all under 15 metres and under 200 horsepower. You can rent them from Estepona port, which sits just west of the famous Puerto Banús, and cruise the Costa del Sol coast without a care.

Estepona port, officially Puerto Deportivo de Estepona, is a quieter alternative to the chaos of Banús. It has 380 berths, a handful of decent chiringuitos, and direct access to the Strait of Gibraltar. You can head east towards Marbella and see La Concha mountain looming, or west towards the dolphin grounds off Tarifa. The licence-free boats we list are mostly RIBs and small motorboats, typically 4 to 7 metres, with 40 to 100 horsepower. They are simple to operate: throttle, steering wheel, kill switch. The briefing covers the no-go zones, the speed limits inside the port, and how to anchor at a beach like Cala del Faro or the Playa del Cristo. You will be out on the water within 30 minutes of arriving.

Booking through BoatHire24 means you see exactly what is available on your chosen date. No phone calls, no emails back and forth. You pick the boat, pick a time slot (usually 2, 4, or 8 hours), and pay online. The price includes fuel, insurance, and the safety equipment. Some operators ask for a refundable deposit of around EUR 300, which you get back if you do not crash into anything. You can also add extras like a cooler bag, snorkel gear, or a paddleboard. The whole point is that you rent a speedboat from Estepona port without a licence, and it takes about five minutes to book.
bodyHtml:
What Does No Licence Mean Exactly

In Spain, you can drive a boat without a licence if the boat has a maximum power of 11.26 kW (about 15 horsepower) and a length under 5 metres. That is the law for the so-called licence-free category. But the boats we list for speedboat hire Estepona port no licence are usually more powerful than that. They are still legal to drive without a licence because they are rented with a skipper who holds a valid licence. The skipper stays on board, but you are the one at the helm. You drive, the skipper supervises. It is a bit like a driving instructor in a dual-control car. You get the thrill of driving a speedboat without needing to pass any exam. The skipper will also handle the mooring and the docking, which is the stressful part anyway.

Some operators in Estepona offer true licence-free boats that you can take out alone. Those are the small day boats with a 15 hp outboard, top speed around 6 knots. They are fine for pottering along the coast but not what most people mean by speedboat. If you want actual speed, 25 to 35 knots, you need the skipper. The good news is that the skipper is included in the price, so you are not paying extra. You are just sharing the boat with one more person who knows what they are doing. The cost for a 4-hour rental of a 6-metre RIB with a skipper is from around EUR 350. For a 2-hour trip, from around EUR 250. That is cheaper than a dinner for two in Puerto Banús.

Estepona Port Location and Facilities

Puerto Deportivo de Estepona is at the western end of the Costa del Sol, about 15 minutes by car from Puerto Banús and 20 minutes from Gibraltar airport. The port has a petrol station, a small supermarket, and several bars and restaurants. You can park in the underground car park for around EUR 2 per hour. The boat hire offices are on the main promenade, near the fishing boat area. The port is sheltered by two breakwaters, so the water inside is calm. The exit channel is straightforward: you head straight out past the green buoy and then turn left or right depending on your planned route.

From Estepona port, you have two main cruising directions. East takes you past the beaches of Cancelada and the Cabopino dunes, then into Marbella bay. West takes you past the Playa del Cristo, the Punta de la Doncella, and then the long stretch of beach towards Manilva and the Gibraltar strait. The wind in this area is usually a light westerly in the morning, picking up to 10-15 knots in the afternoon. The water temperature in summer is around 22-24 degrees Celsius. Visibility is generally good, though the haze from the African dust can reduce it in August. You can also anchor at the Cala del Faro, a small cove with a lighthouse, about 3 nautical miles west of Estepona. It is a popular spot for swimming and snorkelling, and the water is clear enough to see the sandy bottom at 5 metres depth.

Boat Options for Speedboat Hire Estepona Port No Licence

The boats available for speedboat hire Estepona port no licence fall into three categories. First, the RIBs (rigid inflatable boats). These are the most common: a fibreglass hull with inflatable tubes, typically 5 to 7 metres long, with a 60 to 150 hp outboard engine. They are fast, stable, and easy to handle. A 6-metre RIB with a 115 hp engine will do 30 knots with two people on board. Second, the small motorboats, which are more like a traditional fishing boat but with a planing hull. They are slower, around 15-20 knots, but more comfortable for a family with kids. Third, the luxury day boats, which are basically small yachts with a sun pad and a toilet. They have a single inboard engine, around 200 hp, and a top speed of 25 knots. They are more expensive, from around EUR 500 for a half day.

All these boats have a maximum capacity of 6 to 8 people, depending on the model. The RIBs usually take 6, the motorboats take 8, and the luxury day boats take 6. The skipper is included in that number, so the actual passenger count is one less. The boats come with a basic safety kit: life jackets for everyone, a first aid kit, a fire extinguisher, and a VHF radio. The operator will also give you a phone or a handheld radio to contact the base. Some boats have a Bluetooth speaker, so you can play your own music. None of them have a kitchen or a cabin, but you can bring your own food and drink. The cooler bag is a common extra.

Pricing and What Is Included

The price for speedboat hire Estepona port no licence depends on the boat size, the engine power, and the rental duration. A typical 2-hour rental of a 5-metre RIB with a 60 hp engine costs from around EUR 250. A 4-hour rental of a 6-metre RIB with a 115 hp engine costs from around EUR 350. An 8-hour full day rental of a luxury day boat costs from around EUR 600. These prices include the skipper, fuel, insurance, and basic safety equipment. They do not include food, drink, or the refundable deposit. The deposit is usually EUR 200 to EUR 500, paid in cash or by credit card, and returned at the end of the trip if the boat is undamaged. Some operators accept a deposit by bank transfer if you book online.

You can also add extras. A cooler bag with ice costs around EUR 20. Snorkel sets cost around EUR 10 each. A paddleboard costs around EUR 30 for the day. Some operators offer a GoPro rental for EUR 25. The fuel is included in the price, but the operator may limit the engine hours. For example, a 4-hour rental might include 2 hours of engine running time, with the rest spent anchored. If you exceed the limit, you pay extra for fuel at around EUR 1.5 per litre. The boats have a fuel consumption of around 15 to 30 litres per hour at cruising speed, so the included fuel is usually enough for a typical trip.

Best Time of Year for Speedboat Hire Estepona Port

The season for speedboat hire Estepona port no licence runs from April to October. The best months are June, July, August, and September, when the sea is calm and the air temperature is above 25 degrees Celsius. July and August are the busiest, with prices up to 20% higher and availability lower. May and October are quieter, with lower prices and fewer people on the water. The sea conditions in May can be a bit choppy, with a 1-metre swell, but it is still fine for a RIB. In October, the water is still warm enough for swimming, around 20 degrees Celsius. The wind is usually light in the morning, so a morning trip is recommended. The afternoon sea breeze can pick up to 15 knots, which is fine for a RIB but can be uncomfortable on a small motorboat.

The winter months, November to March, are not ideal. The sea is rougher, the air temperature is lower, and many operators close or only offer trips by reservation. A few operators still run, but you will need to check availability. The water temperature drops to 15 degrees Celsius, so swimming is not pleasant. If you are determined to go in winter, choose a larger boat and a shorter trip. The wind can be strong, especially in January and February, when the Levante wind blows from the east. That wind creates a choppy sea in the Strait of Gibraltar, and Estepona is close enough to feel it.

How to Book a Speedboat in Estepona Port Without a Licence

Booking through BoatHire24 search is the simplest method. You go to the search page, select Estepona as the port, choose the date and time, and filter by boat type. You can also filter by price, capacity, and whether the boat is licence-free. The results show the available boats with photos, specifications, and the total price. You click on a boat, read the details, and click book. You pay by credit card or PayPal. The operator confirms the booking within a few hours. You receive a confirmation email with the meeting point, the skipper's name, and the operator's phone number. On the day, you go to the port, find the operator's office, sign a waiver, and get the briefing. Then you are on the water.

If you prefer to book directly with an operator, you can call or email them. But you will not get the same transparency on availability and price. The operators on BoatHire24 have agreed to the same cancellation policy: free cancellation up to 48 hours before the trip, 50% refund up to 24 hours, no refund after that. That is standard. Some operators offer a weather guarantee: if the sea conditions are dangerous, they cancel and refund you fully. That is worth checking before you book. The weather in Estepona is generally good, but the Strait of Gibraltar can produce sudden squalls. The skipper will decide on the day whether it is safe to go out.

Who Is Speedboat Hire Estepona Port No Licence For

This type of hire is for anyone who wants to drive a boat but does not have a licence. It is popular with tourists, expats, and locals who have never tried it. It is also good for groups of friends who want a day on the water without the formality of a yacht charter. The speedboat is not a luxury experience. It is a fun, fast, and slightly wet ride. You will get splashed. You will feel the wind in your face. You will see the coast from a different angle. The skipper will point out the landmarks: the Rock of Gibraltar on a clear day, the mountains of Africa, the dolphins if you are lucky. The dolphins are common in the Strait of Gibraltar, especially in summer. You can see them from Estepona if you go far enough west, about 5 nautical miles. The skipper will know the spots.

The experience is also suitable for families with children. The boats have life jackets in children's sizes. The speed is controllable. The skipper can slow down if anyone feels nervous. The maximum age is not an issue, but children under 12 need a life jacket at all times. The minimum age for driving is 16, with the skipper's permission. Some operators allow 14-year-olds to drive in calm conditions. The boat is not a party boat. You can bring drinks, but the skipper will stop you if you get too drunk. The rules are there for safety. The port authority can fine operators for reckless behaviour, so they are strict. You are there to enjoy the sea, not to cause trouble.

Comparing Estepona to Other Ports for No-Licence Hire

Estepona is not the only port on the Costa del Sol where you can hire a speedboat without a licence. Puerto Banús has more options but higher prices and more traffic. Marbella Marina is quieter but has fewer boats. Cabopino is small and relaxed, with a sandy beach nearby. Benalmádena is further east, closer to Málaga airport, and has a large marina with many operators. Sotogrande is exclusive and expensive, with a golf course and a polo field. Estepona sits in the middle: cheaper than Banús, quieter than Benalmádena, and closer to the dolphin grounds. The port itself is less glamorous, but the water is the same. The boats are the same. The difference is the atmosphere. Estepona is a working port with a few tourist boats. It is not a show-off place. You go there to get on the water quickly and without fuss.

The distance from Marbella town to Estepona port is about 20 minutes by car. From Málaga airport, it is about 50 minutes. From Gibraltar, it is 20 minutes. The port is easy to find, with clear signs from the main road. The parking is adequate. The facilities are basic but sufficient. If you want a more polished experience, go to Puerto Banús. If you want a quieter and cheaper option, go to Estepona. The choice depends on your priorities. For most people, Estepona is the best compromise. The boats are well maintained, the skippers are experienced, and the prices are reasonable. You can read more about the differences on our blog, where we compare the ports in detail.

Safety and Legal Requirements for No-Licence Speedboat Hire

Even though you do not need a licence to drive the boat, you still need to follow the rules. The skipper is responsible for the vessel and will enforce the safety regulations. You must wear a life jacket when the boat is moving at speed. You must stay within the coastal navigation zone, usually 2 nautical miles from the shore. You must not go near the shipping lanes in the Strait of Gibraltar. You must not anchor in protected areas like the Cabopino dunes. The skipper will explain all of this. The boat has a maximum capacity, and you must not exceed it. The engine has a kill switch lanyard that you attach to your life jacket. If you fall overboard, the engine stops. It is simple and effective.

The legal requirement for a licence is waived because the skipper holds a valid Professional Skipper licence (PYC or PER in Spanish terms). The boat is insured for commercial use, and the insurance covers the skipper and the passengers. You are not covered if you drive the boat without the skipper present, so do not try that. The operator will ask for your passport or ID and will keep a copy for their records. They will also ask you to sign a liability waiver. This is standard. The whole process takes 10 minutes. The briefing covers the basics: how to start the engine, how to steer, how to stop, how to anchor, and what to do in an emergency. You will also get a chart of the area with the no-go zones marked. The skipper will be with you the whole time, so you can ask questions.

What to Bring and What to Expect on the Day

Bring sunscreen, a towel, sunglasses, a hat, and a change of clothes. You will get wet. The boat has a small storage compartment for your bag, but it is not waterproof. Bring a waterproof bag for your phone and wallet. The boat does not have a toilet, so use the port facilities before you leave. The chiringuitos on the beach are good for a stop, but you need to anchor and swim ashore. Some operators allow you to beach the boat on a sandy beach, but only if the tide and conditions allow. The skipper will advise. You can bring your own food and drink, but glass bottles are not allowed. Use plastic cups. The boat has a table or a flat area where you can put a picnic. The cooler bag keeps things cold.

The trip itself is straightforward. You leave the port, the skipper shows you how to accelerate and steer, and you head to your chosen destination. The coastline is scenic: the white houses of Estepona old town, the green hills, the blue sea. You can stop at a beach, anchor, swim, and have a drink. The skipper will stay on board or swim nearby. The return trip is the same. You come back to the port, the skipper handles the docking, and you are done. The total time includes the briefing, the trip, and the return. Do not expect to be back exactly on time. The sea and the wind can cause delays. The operator allows a 15-minute grace period. After that, you pay extra. The price is usually EUR 50 per half hour. So plan your trip accordingly.

Why Choose BoatHire24 for Speedboat Hire Estepona Port No Licence

BoatHire24 lists only boats that are available for instant online booking. You see the real availability, not a request form. You compare prices and boats side by side. You read the cancellation policy before you pay. You get a confirmation within minutes. The operators on our platform are vetted for safety and reliability. We do not list boats that are not properly insured or skippered. We also provide a support line if you have any issues on the day. The booking is secure, and your payment is protected. We have been doing this since 2019, and we know the Costa del Sol market well. The speedboat hire Estepona port no licence category is one of our most popular, especially in summer. You can see the reviews on each boat page, written by real customers. They are not fabricated. They are honest.

The alternative is to walk along the port and find an operator on the day. That works, but you might not find the boat you want, and you will pay a walk-in price that is often higher. Booking online gives you certainty and usually a better price. The operators on BoatHire24 offer a price match guarantee: if you find the same boat for less elsewhere, we will match it. That is rare in the boat rental industry. So you can book with confidence. The whole process is designed to be easy, fast, and transparent. You do not need to call anyone. You do not need to speak Spanish. You just book, show up, and drive. That is the point of speedboat hire Estepona port no licence.

faqs.q: Do I need a licence to rent a speedboat in Estepona port?
faqs.a: No, you do not need a licence. The boat comes with a licensed skipper who supervises you at the helm. You drive the boat yourself under their guidance. Some boats are true licence-free day boats that you can take alone, but those are slower and less powerful.

faqs.q: How much does speedboat hire Estepona port no licence cost?
faqs.a: Prices start from around EUR 250 for a 2-hour rental of a 5-metre RIB. A 4-hour rental of a 6-metre RIB costs from around EUR 350. A full day luxury day boat costs from around EUR 600. All prices include the skipper, fuel, and insurance.

faqs.q: Can I bring my own food and drink on the speedboat?
faqs.a: Yes, you can bring your own food and drink. Glass bottles are not allowed for safety reasons. Use plastic cups and containers. Some operators offer a cooler bag with ice for an extra cost of around EUR 20.

faqs.q: What is the maximum number of people on a speedboat?
faqs.a: The maximum capacity is usually 6 to 8 people, including the skipper. A 5-metre RIB takes 6 people, a 6-metre motorboat takes 8, and a luxury day boat takes 6. The exact limit is shown on the boat page.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad on the day of my booking?
faqs.a: The skipper decides if it is safe to go out. If the sea conditions are dangerous, the operator cancels the trip and gives you a full refund. Some operators offer a weather guarantee. Check the cancellation policy before booking.

faqs.q: How far in advance should I book a speedboat in Estepona?
faqs.a: In summer, book at least a week in advance for popular dates. In spring and autumn, a few days is usually enough. Last-minute bookings are possible but the choice of boats is limited. Booking through BoatHire24 shows real-time availability.

faqs.q: Is it safe to drive a speedboat without a licence?
faqs.a: Yes, it is safe because a licensed skipper is on board. They give you a briefing on how to operate the boat and supervise you at all times. The boat has safety equipment including life jackets, a kill switch, and a VHF radio. The skipper handles the docking and navigation.

title: Luxury Catamaran Rental Puerto Banus Weekend: Escape
metaDescription: Book a luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend with a skipper. Escape the crowds on a private charter from Marbella to Cala del Faro. Prices from around EUR 200 per person.
h1: Luxury Catamaran Rental Puerto Banus Weekend: Your Escape
keyword: luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend
intro:
You want a luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend escape. You want to leave the packed beach clubs and the endless noise behind, float on calm Mediterranean water, and drink something cold while someone else steers. That is exactly what you get.

Every charter on BoatHire24 includes a licensed skipper, so you do not need a licence or any experience. You just turn up at Puerto Banús, step aboard, and the crew handles the rest. The boat is yours for the weekend, or for a day, or for a few hours. You choose.

This page covers everything you need to know about renting a luxury catamaran from Puerto Banús for a weekend escape: what is included, which ports you can leave from, what the boats look like, how much it costs, and how to book without stress.
bodyHtml:
What a Weekend Escape on a Catamaran Actually Looks Like

A luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend is not a booze cruise. It is a private charter with space, stability, and style. Catamarans have two hulls, so they do not lean like monohull yachts. You can walk around, eat, nap, and even read a book without feeling seasick. That matters if you have guests who are not used to the sea.

The typical weekend itinerary: leave Puerto Banús on Saturday morning, head east towards Cala del Faro or Marbella Marina, drop anchor for lunch and swimming, then cruise back in the afternoon. Some charters offer overnight stays, so you sleep on the boat and wake up in a quiet cove. Sunday you can go west towards Estepona or even the Strait of Gibraltar if you have a longer charter.

Catamarans in this category range from 40 to 60 feet. They have a flybridge with sun loungers, a shaded cockpit, a full kitchen (galley), and cabins with en-suite bathrooms. You can bring your own food and drink, or ask the crew to cater. Most boats have a sound system, a paddleboard, and snorkelling gear.

Important: the skipper lives on board for multi-day charters. That is standard. They have their own cabin and do not intrude on your space. They are there to navigate, handle mooring, and prepare the boat. You do not have to tip, but it is common to give 10-15% if the service is good.

Puerto Banús: The Starting Point

Puerto Banús is the most famous marina on the Costa del Sol. It is in Marbella, about 10 minutes from the town centre. The marina is full of superyachts, designer shops, and expensive restaurants. It is also the best place to start a BoatHire24 catamaran charter.

Why Puerto Banús? Because the port is sheltered, the facilities are excellent, and the departure is smooth. You walk from the car park to the boat in five minutes. The crew will meet you at the gangway and take your bags. You do not queue, you do not wait.

The coastline east of Puerto Banús is the best for a weekend escape. You have the Sierra Blanca mountains behind you, La Concha peak visible from the water, and a string of small beaches and coves. The water is clear, and the wind is usually light in the morning.

If you want a different departure port, you can also charter from Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo), Cabopino, Estepona, Sotogrande, or Benalmádena. Each has its own character, but Puerto Banús is the most convenient for a luxury weekend.

What Boats Are Available for a Luxury Catamaran Rental Puerto Banus Weekend

The catamarans on BoatHire24 are mostly from Lagoon, Fountaine Pajot, and Bali. These are production builders that make reliable, comfortable boats. You will not get a bare-bones charter. Every boat has air conditioning (essential in summer), a generator, and a watermaker. The interior is modern, with wood finishes and leather upholstery.

Typical models: Lagoon 42, Lagoon 46, Fountaine Pajot Astrea 42, Bali 4.6. These are 4-6 cabin boats, but for a weekend charter you will only use 2-3 cabins. The extra cabins are locked off or used by the crew. You get the master cabin with a double bed and ensuite, plus one or two guest cabins.

The flybridge is the social centre. It has a helm station, a sunbed area, and a table for dining. The cockpit is shaded and has a second dining area. The trampolines at the front are for sunbathing. The swim platform at the back is low to the water, so getting in and out is easy.

RIB speedboats and motor yachts are also available if you want a faster, more sporty experience, but for a weekend escape, a catamaran is the best choice. It is stable, spacious, and fuel-efficient. You can cover 20-30 nautical miles in a day without refuelling.

How Much Does a Luxury Catamaran Rental Puerto Banus Weekend Cost

Pricing for a luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend varies by season, boat size, and duration. You can expect to pay from around EUR 2,500 to EUR 6,000 for a 3-day weekend (Friday to Sunday) in high season (June to September). Low season (October to May) is cheaper, from around EUR 1,800 to EUR 4,000.

That price includes the boat, the skipper, basic insurance, and fuel for a standard itinerary. It does not include food, drink, port fees (if you overnight in a different marina), or a damage deposit (usually EUR 1,000-2,000, refundable).

If you want a crewed option with a hostess or a chef, add EUR 200-400 per day. If you want to go further, like to the Strait of Gibraltar to see dolphins, fuel costs increase. The crew will give you a quote before you book.

For a group of 6-8 people, the per-person cost is roughly EUR 200-300 per person per day. That is competitive with a hotel room and a restaurant dinner, but you get a private boat and a skipper.

Check the search page for real-time availability and prices. You can filter by boat type, port, and date.

Best Time for a Weekend Catamaran Charter on the Costa del Sol

The Mediterranean season runs from April to October. July and August are the busiest and most expensive. The water temperature reaches 24-26 degrees C. The air temperature hits 30-35 degrees C. The wind is light in the morning and picks up in the afternoon (the Poniente wind from the west). That is fine for catamarans, which handle chop well.

May and June are the sweet spot. The weather is warm (22-28 degrees C), the water is cooler but swimmable (18-22 degrees C), and the marinas are less crowded. September and October are also good, with fewer tourists and lower prices. The water stays warm until mid-October.

Winter charters (November to March) are possible but less popular. The weather is cooler (15-20 degrees C), and the sea can be rough. Some boats are laid up for maintenance. If you want a winter escape, look for a motor yacht with heating, not an open catamaran.

What to Do on a Weekend Catamaran Charter

A luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend is about relaxing, but you can also plan activities. Here are the best options:

Cala del Faro. This is a small cove east of Marbella, near the lighthouse. The water is calm, and there are no beach clubs. You anchor, swim, paddleboard, and have lunch. It is the most popular stop for day charters.

Estepona Old Town. If you head west from Puerto Banús, you reach Estepona in about 45 minutes. The marina is small but charming. You can tie up for a few hours, walk into the old town for tapas, and come back. The old town has whitewashed buildings and flower pots on every street.

Dolphin watching in the Strait of Gibraltar. This is a longer trip, about 2 hours from Puerto Banús. The Strait is a migration route for dolphins and whales. You can see common dolphins, striped dolphins, and sometimes pilot whales. The crew will know the best spots. This trip uses more fuel, so check the price.

Nerja Caves. If you have a full weekend, you can cruise east past Marbella to Nerja. The caves are a 45-minute drive from the port, but you can anchor off Nerja and take a taxi. The caves are a major attraction, with stalactites and stalagmites. Not all charters offer this, so ask.

Dinner at sea. You can anchor off Puerto Banús at sunset and have dinner catered on board. The crew will set up the table, serve the food, and clean up. You watch the lights of the marina while you eat. That is the definition of an escape.

Who Is This For

A luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend is for groups of friends, families with older children, couples, or corporate groups. It is not for large parties with loud music and heavy drinking. The skipper has the right to refuse boarding if guests are intoxicated or aggressive.

It is also for people who want privacy. You do not share the boat with strangers. It is a private charter, so you control the itinerary, the music, and the food. If you want to stop for a swim, you tell the skipper. If you want to stay longer at a cove, you stay.

If you have never been on a catamaran before, do not worry. The skipper will give a safety briefing and show you around. The boat is stable, so you will not feel sick. Bring sunscreen, a hat, sunglasses, and a light jacket for the evening. The crew provides towels and snorkelling gear.

How to Book a Luxury Catamaran Rental Puerto Banus Weekend on BoatHire24

Booking is straightforward. Go to the search page, select Puerto Banús as the port, choose catamaran as the boat type, and pick your dates. You will see a list of available boats with photos, specs, and prices. Each boat has a description and a list of inclusions.

You can send an enquiry directly through the site. The owner or the charter manager will respond within a few hours. They will confirm availability, answer your questions, and send a booking contract. Payment is usually 50% deposit and 50% before departure. You can pay by bank transfer or credit card.

Read the cancellation policy before you book. Most charters offer a full refund if you cancel 30 days before departure. Closer to the date, you may lose the deposit. Some owners offer a free date change if you rebook within the same season.

If you want advice, check the blog for guides on catamaran charters, itineraries, and packing lists. The blog is updated regularly with local tips.

Why Book Through BoatHire24

BoatHire24 is a marketplace for boat rentals and yacht charters on the Costa del Sol. We list boats from local owners and charter companies. Every boat is inspected, and every charter includes a licensed skipper. We do not charge booking fees. You pay the same price as booking direct.

We also provide support before and during your charter. If you have a problem, you can contact us. We will mediate between you and the owner. That is the advantage of booking through a platform rather than a random website.

The boats on BoatHire24 are real. The photos are recent. The descriptions are accurate. If a boat says it has air conditioning and a sound system, it does. If it says it sleeps 8, it sleeps 8. We do not allow fake listings.

So if you want a luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend escape, start on the BoatHire24 homepage. Search, compare, and book. The sea is waiting.

Why a Catamaran Beats a Motor Yacht for a Weekend Escape

You might think a motor yacht is the obvious choice for a luxury weekend. It is faster and looks flashier. But for a three-day charter, a catamaran has clear advantages. The beam (width) is almost double that of a monohull of the same length. A 46-foot catamaran has the deck space of a 60-foot motor yacht. You get two separate social areas: the flybridge and the shaded cockpit. Everyone can find their own spot without crowding.

Fuel consumption is another factor. A catamaran burns roughly 30-40 litres per hour at cruising speed (8-10 knots). A motor yacht of similar size burns 80-120 litres per hour. For a weekend covering 50-60 nautical miles, that saves you EUR 200-400 in fuel costs. The boat is also quieter. You can hold a conversation on the flybridge without shouting. And if you want to anchor overnight, the shallow draft (1.2-1.5 metres) means you can get closer to beaches than a deep-keel motor yacht.

The only downside is speed. You cruise at 8-10 knots instead of 20-30 knots. That is fine for a weekend escape. You are not racing anywhere. The journey is part of the experience.

Port Options Beyond Puerto Banús

Puerto Banús is the obvious start point, but you have alternatives. Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is 2 nautical miles east. It is smaller and quieter. Parking is cheaper. If you want a more relaxed departure, start here. The marina has a fuel dock and a chandlery. You can provision at the nearby Mercadona supermarket before boarding.

Cabopino is 5 nautical miles east of Marbella. It is a small, sandy-bottom marina near the Artola dunes. It is less commercial than Puerto Banús. The bar on the beach is casual. If you want to avoid the glitz and just get on the water, Cabopino works.

Estepona Marina is 15 nautical miles west of Puerto Banús. It is modern and clean, with a promenade of restaurants. The old town is a 10-minute walk. Estepona is the best port for a weekend heading west towards Gibraltar. Sotogrande is further west, 25 nautical miles from Puerto Banús. It is an exclusive residential marina with a golf course. The port is quiet and secure. Benalmádena is 30 nautical miles east, near Málaga airport. It is a large marina with good facilities. Use it if you are flying into Málaga and want to start immediately.

Each port has its own character. The price of the charter is the same regardless of departure port. The skipper will reposition the boat if you book in advance.

Weekend Itinerary: A Real Example

Here is a concrete itinerary for a luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend. Day one: board at 10:00 at Puerto Banús. Stow your bags. The skipper gives a 15-minute safety briefing. You cast off at 10:30. Head east towards Cala del Faro, 8 nautical miles away. Arrive at 11:30. Anchor in 5 metres of crystal water. Swim, paddleboard, snorkel. Lunch on board at 13:00. The crew serves paella and a bottle of Albariño. At 15:00, you weigh anchor and cruise to Marbella Marina, 2 nautical miles west. Tie up at the visitor pontoon. Walk into Marbella old town for shopping or a drink. Dinner at a restaurant on the Golden Mile. Return to the boat by 23:00. Sleep in the master cabin with air conditioning.

Day two: wake up at 08:00. Coffee and pastries on the flybridge. Cast off at 09:30. Head west towards Estepona, 15 nautical miles. Pass the Sierra Blanca mountains. Spot the La Concha peak. Arrive at Estepona at 11:30. Anchor off the beach. Swim to the shore and walk through the old town. Lunch at a tapas bar on the Plaza de las Flores. Return to the boat at 15:00. Cruise back to Puerto Banús, arriving at 17:00. Disembark at 18:00. That is a full, relaxed weekend without rushing.

If you want to overnight at anchor instead of a marina, the skipper will find a sheltered cove. You will need to plan meals and water usage. The boat has a generator and watermaker, so you are self-sufficient for 48 hours.

Pricing Breakdown: What You Actually Pay

The headline price for a luxury catamaran rental Puerto Banus weekend is EUR 2,500-6,000. But you need to budget for extras. Port fees for overnight stays are EUR 30-80 per night, depending on the marina. Food and drink for 6 people for two days: EUR 200-400 if you cater yourself, EUR 400-800 if you order catering. The crew will provision for you if you send a shopping list in advance.

The damage deposit is EUR 1,000-2,000, refunded within 7 days if there is no damage. You pay this by credit card or bank transfer. Some owners offer a waiver for EUR 100-200, which covers minor damage like broken glasses or stained cushions. Fuel is included for a standard itinerary of 50-60 nautical miles. If you go further, you pay the difference at EUR 1.50-2.00 per litre.

Gratuity is optional but common. EUR 100-200 per crew member for a weekend is fair. The skipper and hostess split this. You give it in cash at the end.

Compare this to a hotel: a 5-star room in Marbella costs EUR 300-800 per night. Two rooms for two nights is EUR 1,200-3,200. Add dinner for 6 at a restaurant: EUR 300-600. You are at EUR 1,500-3,800 without any boat time. The catamaran gives you accommodation, transport, and entertainment in one package. The value is clear.

What to Pack for a Weekend Catamaran Charter

You need less than you think. The boat has towels, bedding, snorkelling gear, and basic toiletries. Pack: swimwear (2-3 pairs), a light jumper or hoodie for the evening (the wind drops at sunset), non-slip shoes (no black soles that mark the deck), sunglasses with a strap, reef-safe sunscreen (SPF 50+), a hat with a chin strap, a waterproof phone pouch, and a small dry bag for your phone and wallet.

Do not bring suitcases. Bring duffel bags or soft luggage. They are easier to stow in the cabins. The crew will tell you where to put them. Do not bring glass bottles. The boat uses plastic or stainless steel for safety. If you want to bring your own alcohol, check with the owner. Some allow it, some prefer you buy from their bar at cost.

If you are prone to seasickness, bring medication. The catamaran is stable, but if you are sensitive, take a half-dose of travel sickness pills before departure. The skipper can also adjust the speed and course to minimise motion.

faqs.q: Do I need a licence to rent a luxury catamaran in Puerto Banus for the weekend?
faqs.a: No. Every charter on BoatHire24 includes a licensed skipper. You do not need any licence or experience. The skipper handles navigation, mooring, and safety. You just relax and enjoy.

faqs.q: Can I sleep overnight on the catamaran during a weekend charter?
faqs.a: Yes. Most luxury catamarans have cabins with en-suite bathrooms. You can stay overnight on the boat at anchor or in a marina. The skipper stays on board too, in their own cabin. Overnight charters usually require a minimum of 2-3 days.

faqs.q: What is included in the price of a weekend catamaran rental?
faqs.a: The price includes the boat, the skipper, basic insurance, and fuel for a standard itinerary. It does not include food, drink, port fees, or a damage deposit. Some charters include paddleboards and snorkelling gear. Check the listing for details.

faqs.q: How many people can fit on a luxury catamaran for a weekend?
faqs.a: Typical catamarans for charter sleep 6-8 guests in 3-4 cabins. The maximum number of passengers on board during the day is usually 12, including the crew. For a weekend with overnight stays, the limit is the number of berths, which is usually 8-10.

faqs.q: What is the best destination for a weekend catamaran trip from Puerto Banus?
faqs.a: Cala del Faro is the most popular stop. It is a calm cove east of Marbella, perfect for swimming and lunch. For a longer trip, head west to Estepona old town or south to the Strait of Gibraltar to see dolphins. The crew can advise based on weather and your preferences.

faqs.q: Can I bring my own food and drink on the catamaran?
faqs.a: Yes. You can bring whatever you want. The boat has a full kitchen with a fridge, freezer, and stove. The crew can help prepare and serve. Some charters offer catering at an extra cost. Alcohol is allowed, but the skipper will refuse service if anyone is intoxicated.

faqs.q: What happens if the weather is bad on my weekend charter?
faqs.a: The skipper makes the final call on safety. If the weather is dangerous, the charter may be postponed or rerouted to a sheltered area. Most charters have a weather clause in the contract. You will not be charged for a cancellation due to unsafe conditions.

title: Private Boat Charter Marbella Stag: Ultimate Party
metaDescription: Looking for a private boat charter Marbella stag party? We've got the real cost, the best ports, and what to expect. No fluff, just facts. Book your trip now.
h1: Private Boat Charter Marbella Stag: The Real Deal
intro:
If you're planning a stag do in Marbella and someone has suggested a private boat charter, you're probably picturing a day of sun, sea, and questionable decisions. Good news: that's exactly what you get. But there's a lot of noise online about what's included, what it costs, and whether it's actually worth the money. We're BoatHire24, a marketplace for boat rentals and yacht charters on the Costa del Sol, and we're here to cut through the crap. Every charter we list includes a licensed skipper, so no one has to pretend they know how to dock a 40-foot motor yacht after three beers.

This page is for you if you're organising a stag party and want a straight answer: what does a private boat charter Marbella stag actually involve, how much does it realistically cost, and which port should you depart from. We'll cover the boat types, the ports, the hidden costs, and the legal bits you'd rather not think about but definitely should. No fluff, no sales pitch, just the facts. And if you want to see what's available right now, head to our search page to filter by boat type, capacity, and port.
bodyHtml:
What a Private Boat Charter Marbella Stag Actually Includes

Let's start with what you're actually paying for. A private boat charter means the entire vessel is yours for the duration. No other groups, no strangers, just your mates and the crew. The crew typically consists of a licensed skipper (mandatory for all our charters) and sometimes a host or stewardess depending on the boat size. The skipper handles navigation, safety, and docking. They do not handle your drinks, so if you want a dedicated bartender, you need to request that separately.

Most charters include fuel for a set number of hours, usually 4 to 8 hours, and basic insurance. What they don't include is food and drink, though many operators offer catering packages at an extra cost. You can bring your own booze, but check with the operator first because some have corkage fees or restrictions on glass bottles. Also, expect a security deposit, usually refundable, but it can be held against damage or excessive mess. For a stag group, that deposit might be higher than usual, so ask upfront.

All charters include life jackets, a first aid kit, and basic safety briefing. Some boats have sound systems, some don't. If music is non-negotiable, confirm the boat has Bluetooth or aux input. Don't assume. And if you want water toys like a paddleboard or a towable inflatable, those are often add-ons. The base price is for the boat and the skipper. Everything else is extra.

Which Port to Depart From for Your Stag Boat Charter

Marbella and the Costa del Sol have several departure ports. Your choice affects the scenery, the distance to open water, and the vibe. Here's the breakdown.

Puerto Banús is the obvious choice for a stag party. It's flashy, full of supercars and designer shops, and the marina itself is a spectacle. Departing from here puts you in the heart of the action. You'll cruise past the famous marina, see the luxury yachts, and head towards the coast. The downside is that it's busy, and mooring fees are higher, which can inflate the charter price. But if you want the full Marbella experience, this is it.

Marbella Marina (Puerto Deportivo) is more laid back. It's closer to the old town, so you can combine a boat trip with a wander through the orange-tree-lined streets. The marina is smaller and less crowded, which means quicker departure and potentially lower costs. Good option if your group wants a balance of boat time and land time.

Cabopino is a small, quiet marina near the dunes of Artola. It's less glamorous but more relaxed. If your stag group is more about chilling and less about showing off, this works. The water is clear, and you're close to some nice coves for swimming.

Estepona offers a more authentic Andalusian vibe. The old town is beautiful, and the marina is modern but not pretentious. Departing from here gives you easy access to the coast towards Gibraltar, where you might spot dolphins. It's a bit further from Marbella centre, so factor in transport.

Sotogrande is upscale but quieter. It's a polo and golf crowd, so the marina is polished but not party central. Good if you want a more exclusive feel, but probably not the first choice for a stag unless you're staying nearby.

Benalmádena is a bit further east, near Torremolinos. It's a large marina with plenty of boat options, and it's close to the airport. If your group is flying in and wants to go straight to the boat, this is convenient. The coast here is more built up, but you can still find nice spots to anchor.

For a stag, Puerto Banús is the default. But if you want to save money or avoid the crowds, consider Marbella Marina or Cabopino. Check our BoatHire24 homepage for a quick overview of all ports.

Boat Options for a Stag Party: What Actually Works

Not every boat is suitable for a stag do. Here's what's available and what to pick based on your group size and style.

Motor Yachts: These are the most common choice for stag parties. They're fast, spacious, and have plenty of deck space for socialising. Sizes range from 30 feet (fits 8-10 people) to 60 feet (fits 20+). Motor yachts have a flybridge, which is the upper deck with a helm station and seating. That's where the party usually happens. They also have a cabin below with a toilet and sometimes a small kitchen. For a day charter, you don't need cabins, but having a toilet is essential. Motor yachts are louder and consume more fuel, which can affect the price if you're paying for fuel separately.

Catamarans: Catamarans are stable, spacious, and have a shallow draft, meaning you can get closer to the shore. They have two hulls, so the deck area is huge. They're great for groups because there's no rolling motion, so less seasickness. The downside is they're slower than motor yachts. If your stag group wants to cover distance and see the coast, a catamaran might feel sluggish. But if you plan to anchor in a cove and swim, it's ideal. Catamarans often have a trampoline net at the front, which is fun for lounging but not for jumping off.

Sailing Yachts: Unless your group is full of sailors, avoid sailing yachts for a stag. They heel (tilt) when sailing, which can be uncomfortable, and the deck space is limited. You also have to deal with ropes and sails, which get in the way. Some sailing yachts have a motor for when there's no wind, but the experience is not party-friendly. Save these for a more mature group.

RIBs and Speedboats: These are small, fast, and open. They're fun for a short trip, like a 2-hour blast along the coast, but not for a full day. They have limited shade and no toilet. For a stag, they work as an add-on activity, not the main event. Maximum capacity is usually 8-10 people.

Licence-Free Day Boats: These are small motorboats (up to 15 hp) that you can drive without a boating licence. They're typically 4-6 metres and fit 4-6 people. For a stag, they're too small unless it's an intimate group of close mates. You also have to handle the boat yourself, which adds stress.

Fishing Boats: If your stag group is into fishing, you can charter a fishing boat. These are equipped with rods, bait, and fish finders. The skipper will take you to productive spots. But the focus is fishing, not partying. If you want to combine both, you'll need a larger motor yacht that allows fishing on the side.

Superyachts: For a truly extravagant stag, superyachts are available. These are over 24 metres and come with a full crew, including a chef, stewardesses, and sometimes a jet ski. Prices are on request, and you're looking at EUR 10,000 or more for a day. They're rare for stags, but if you have the budget, go for it.

For a typical stag group of 10-15 people, a 40-50 foot motor yacht from Puerto Banús is the sweet spot. It's big enough to party, small enough to feel private. Browse our search page to see what's available for your dates.

Pricing Guidance for a Private Boat Charter Marbella Stag

Let's talk money. Prices vary wildly based on boat size, season, duration, and port. Here's a realistic range.

For a half-day (4 hours) on a 40-foot motor yacht in low season (October to April), expect from around EUR 1,200 to EUR 1,800. In high season (June to September), that same boat jumps to EUR 2,000 to EUR 3,000. Full-day (8 hours) in high season can be EUR 3,500 to EUR 5,000. Catamarans are similar, sometimes slightly cheaper because they use less fuel. RIBs are cheaper, around EUR 600 to EUR 1,000 for 4 hours.

These prices are for the boat and skipper only. Add-ons increase the cost. Catering packages range from EUR 30 to EUR 80 per person for food and drinks. Open bar packages (beer, wine, soft drinks) can be EUR 40 to EUR 60 per person. If you bring your own booze, some operators charge a corkage fee of EUR 10 to EUR 20 per bottle. Water toys like a paddleboard or inflatable banana cost extra, typically EUR 100 to EUR 200 per item.

Security deposits are common, ranging from EUR 500 to EUR 2,000. This is refunded if the boat is returned in good condition. For stag groups, deposits are often at the higher end. Also, some operators charge a cleaning fee if the boat is left messy, usually EUR 100 to EUR 300. Ask about this before booking.

Tips are not included. It's customary to tip the skipper and crew 10-15% of the charter fee if you're happy. For a EUR 2,000 charter, that's EUR 200 to EUR 300. Factor that into your budget.

To get an exact quote, you need to contact the operator with your group size, preferred date, and duration. We don't list live prices because they change with demand. But the numbers above give you a baseline. If a quote seems too low, ask what's excluded. If it seems too high, negotiate or consider a different port or boat type.

Best Time of Year for a Stag Boat Charter in Marbella

The Costa del Sol has a Mediterranean climate, meaning hot summers and mild winters. The boating season runs from April to October, with peak in July and August.

June, July, August, September: These are the hottest months, with average highs of 28-32 degrees C. Sea temperature is 22-26 degrees C. Perfect for swimming. But these are also the busiest and most expensive months. Book at least 4-6 weeks in advance. The sea is usually calm, but afternoon winds can pick up, especially in August. If your group is prone to seasickness, stick to catamarans or larger motor yachts.

April, May, October: These shoulder months are still warm (20-26 degrees C) but less crowded. Prices are lower, and availability is better. Sea temperature is cooler (16-20 degrees C), so swimming might be brief. But you'll have more space and less competition for popular anchorages like Cala del Faro. Good for a stag that's more about cruising and less about swimming.

November to March: Technically you can charter year-round, but it's not ideal for a stag. Temperatures are 15-20 degrees C, and there's a higher chance of rain and wind. The sea can be choppy. Most operators run reduced schedules. If you're set on a winter stag, choose a motor yacht with a fully enclosed saloon and heating. But honestly, pick a different activity.

For a classic stag party, aim for June or September. The weather is great, the water is warm, and the prices are slightly lower than July-August peak.

How to Book a Private Boat Charter for a Stag Party

Booking through our marketplace is straightforward. Here's the process.

First, go to our search page and enter your group size, preferred date, and port. You'll see a list of available boats with photos, specs, and the operator's contact details. Each listing shows the maximum capacity, which is strictly enforced for insurance reasons. Don't try to sneak extra people on board; the skipper will turn you away.

Once you find a boat you like, click through to the listing. There you'll find a detailed description, what's included, and the operator's terms. Some operators allow instant online booking with a deposit, while others require you to contact them for availability. We recommend contacting at least three operators to compare prices and packages. Ask specifically about stag groups: some operators have policies against them or charge a supplement.

When you're ready to book, you'll typically pay a deposit (30-50%) to secure the date, with the balance due a week before departure. Payment is usually by bank transfer or credit card. Make sure you get a confirmation in writing with the boat name, skipper contact, departure time, and meeting point. Also, confirm the cancellation policy. Most operators offer a full refund if you cancel 14 days or more in advance, but less than that and you might lose your deposit.

On the day, arrive at the marina 15 minutes early. The skipper will do a safety briefing and check IDs. Everyone needs to bring a valid ID (passport or driving licence). The skipper has the right to refuse boarding if anyone is visibly intoxicated or aggressive. Don't be that group.

After the charter, you'll be asked to settle any extras (catering, fuel if not included, damage) and tip the crew. Then you're free to stumble back to your hotel or continue the party on land.

Who Is a Private Boat Charter Marbella Stag For?

This is not for every stag group. It works best for groups of 8 to 20 people who want a shared experience that's a bit different from the usual pub crawl or club night. It's for groups that value privacy and flexibility: you control the music, the drinks, the route, and the pace. If your group is more about drinking games and loud music, a motor yacht with a good sound system is perfect. If they prefer chilling and swimming, a catamaran is better.

It's also for groups that can handle the logistics. You need to coordinate transport to the marina, agree on a budget, and respect the crew. If your group is disorganised or has a history of causing trouble, a boat charter might end in disaster. Operators talk to each other, and if you get a reputation, you'll struggle to book in the future.

Finally, it's for groups that appreciate value over price. A boat charter is not cheap, but when you split the cost among 10-15 people, it becomes reasonable. You get a full day of private entertainment, a skipper, and a unique perspective of the coast. Compare that to buying a table at a club for the same money, and the boat often wins.

If you're still unsure, read our blog for more tips on planning a stag do in Marbella, including what to pack and how to avoid common mistakes.

Legal and Safety Considerations for Stag Groups

You might not want to think about this, but it matters. Spanish maritime law requires all passengers to have a valid ID on board. The skipper is responsible for the safety of everyone, and they can terminate the charter early if they feel unsafe. That includes if the group is too drunk, aggressive, or ignoring safety instructions.

There is no legal drinking age on a private charter because it's not a public space, but the skipper can refuse to serve alcohol to anyone who is already intoxicated. Also, open containers of alcohol are allowed on board, but glass bottles are often banned because broken glass on deck is a hazard. Use plastic cups and cans.

Swimming is allowed only when the boat is anchored and the skipper gives the go-ahead. Never jump off the boat while it's moving. And if you're swimming near rocks or in a cove, watch for boat traffic. The skipper will advise on safe spots.

If someone gets seasick, the skipper usually has medication on board, but bring your own if you're prone. The best prevention is to stay on deck, focus on the horizon, and avoid alcohol on an empty stomach.

Finally, respect the marine environment. Don't throw rubbish overboard. Some coves are protected areas, and fines for littering can be steep. Leave the place as you found it.
